The assumption of equality was entirely a fiction of artificial legislation, and as it led to great abuses, it was time enough to correct the mischief by a return to the old conservative native traditions in favour of the protection of the weaker party.123

In the article The Deccan Agriculturists Bill', published in the Journal of thc Poona Sarvajanik Sabha in October 1879, Ranade defended nearly all the important provisions of the Bill.

Many of the nationalist newspapers of Bombay, however, disapproved of the Act mainly on the ground that it was a half-hearted measure and would not, therefore, succeed in saving the peasant; it dealt with the secondary evil of the moneylender, while leaving the primary evil of a rigid and harsh land revenue system untouched. It could only result in destroying the credit of the peasant, while keeping intact his liabilities, and, therefore, in his further ruin.124 Perhaps working in the minds of the critics of the Act was the feeling, which was openly voiced by the Mahratta of 3 February 1884, that it was ‘a measure that was launched for concealing the real source of the fountain of misery ...a measure by the aid of which the Government wanted to throw off its own responsibilities on to the shoulders of the sowcars'. Even Ranade had qualified his full-throated support to the Act with the statement that it 'could bring relief to the ryot only if it was worked ‘in conjunction with a more liberal land revenue policy, and on that condition alonc'.125 Otherwise, he had further asserted, 'the

Act alone will do no real, tangible, or lasting good, and might even aggravate the present condition of things,'-since the peasant would have to find money somewhere in order to pay the excessive land revenue.126 A few of the Indian leaders also held it against the Act that it tended to ignore the interests of the, moneylender altogether and to effect his complete ruin.127

## VI. RESTRICTIONS ON ALIENATION OF LAND

After 1879, the government took no major step against the growing menace of rural indebtedness till the close of the century when the authorities, taking alarm at the speed with which land was passing into the hands of the non-cultivating. classes, felt compelled to seek out a more drastic remedy than was tried hitherto. The official thinking on this matter had slowly veered round, over the years, to the view that the primary causes of the large-scale transfer of land from the peasantry to the non-cultivating classes were the excessive leniency of the government demandwhich by absorbing only a: part of the economic rent left a large unearned surplus in the hands of the landowners and enabled a chain of parasitic rentreceivers .to exist the unrestricted power of transferring their land by sale or mortgage conferred upon the landholders.

by the British administration, and the thriftless nature of the Indian peasant, who was induced, because of the power of alienation of land vested in him, to borrow heedlessly to the maximum extent of his credit.128 Believing, therefore, that higher the assessment the better for the ryot, some of the British administrators would have perhaps liked to propose an increase in the land revenue in order to make non-cultivating landlordism uneconomic if they had not realiséd that such a proposal was an impossibility politically.129 The only other remedy, it was felt by the authorities, was to take away from the improvident and shortsighted peasant the 'fatal gift' of free alienation of land and to impose restrictions on his power of transferring land and thus to contract his credit and remove the power and temptation to incur large debts.13o

The first major embodiment of the new policy was the Punjab Alienation Act of 19oo. The Act prohibited the permanent alienation of land by a hereditary cultivator except to defined agriculturists without the sanction of a Deputy Commissioner, permitted certain forimns of temporary alienation to non-agriculturists up to a maximum period of 2o years after which the land reverted unencumbered to the alienator, and prohibited the sale of agricultural land in execution of any decree or order.131 It is obvious that the Act was desigued not so much to relieve the existing indebtedness of the peasant or to check its future growth as to prevent the evil of the peasantry of the Punjab being expropriated by the moneylender --an outsider—and thereby perhaps constituting ‘an ever increasing political danger'.132 That is the reason why the transfer of land to agricultural clases was freely permitted; and, in fact, alienation of land was restricted and regulated and not prohibited altogether. It was, of course, expected that the Act would indirectly tend to curb the peasant's tendency and capacity to run to the moneylender at the first temptation.

The next important step in this direction was taken in Bombay in 19o1 with the enactment of the Land Revenue Amendment Act, which contained provisions empowering the government to settle waste lands and fields, which had been forfeited to the government because of default in the payment of land revenue, on a new kind of tenure that carried no right of transfer. Moreover, the Act provided for the grant of forfeited land on such terms and for such periods as the collector might prescribe.133 Because the Act was only an enabling and not an obligatory measure and because it imposed inalienability only on the forfeited land, it might be said to be much narrower in its scope than its Punjab counterpart. On the other hand, it was more stringent than the Punjab Act in two respects: one, that whatever land it was extended to it imposed not restrictions but complete prohibition on alienation of land by sale or mortgage; and two, that it empowered the government to let out forfeited land on short leases in place of the permanent occupancy of the survey tenure.

The nationalist attitude towards the efforts at restricting the power of private alienation of land highlights the differences between the British officials and the Indian leaders regarding the causes of, and the cure for, rural indebtedness. In view of these differences, it was not unexpected that the latter opposed the remedy of non-alienability of land from its very incubatory period to the stage of its acceptance by the Government of India.134

The Punjab Land Alienation Bill was either opposed or left unsupported outside the Punjab.135 Within the Punjab the Bill caused a split in the nationalist ranks;136 it placed many of the Indian nationalists on the horns of a dilemma. For example, the leading Urdu language nationalist paper of the province, the Akhbar-i-Am, in the beginning adopted a very hesitant approach and reacted by opening its columns to communications from both the supporters and the opponents of the measure. After having sat on the fence for nearly a year, it opposed the Bill in its issues dated 7 August 19oo and 27 October 190o.137 But, two weeks later, it turned a somersault and confidently predicted in its issue dated io November 19oo that the Act would prove,a success and all objections to it unjustified.138 In any case, opposition to the Punjab Alienation

Act was not stubborn or heated or prolonged whether in or outside the Punjab.

The Bombay Land Revenue Amendment Bill, on the other hand, aroused great public indignation in the Bombay Presidency. Meetings of protest were held all over the Presidency, and the Bombay Presidency Association, the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha, and the Deccan Sabha sent petitions against it.139 Leading nationalist newspapers filled their columns with vituperations against the Bill,140 while leading public men lashed out against it.141 Some of the Indian members of the Bombay Legislative Council, namely, P. M. Mehta, G. K. Gokhale, G. K. Parekh, Balachandra Krishua, and D. A. Khare, took the unprecedented step of staging what was perhaps the first walk-out in India's legislative history as a protest against the 'hasty' enactment of the Bill.142

The opposition of the Indian national leaders to the nonalienation legislation originated in the main from their belief that, even though such legislation was undertaken with the worthy object of saving the peasant from being expropriated by the moneylender, it would do little good in practice and would probably even go counter to the best interest of the peasantry. Firstly, they averred that restrictions on land alienation would reduce, if not ruin entirely, the ryot's credit, and since he required loans to carry on agricultural operations, to pay the government dues, and to maintain his family during daýs of scarcity this would mean that he would either fail to raise a loan or would be able to do so at much higher rates of interest.143 They further claimed that such restrictions would inevitably lead to a reduction in the value of land,144 that they amounted to an encroachment on the proprietary rights of the peasant, and they would in due course transform him into a virtual serf of the state.145 This criticism was voiced partiçularly loudly by Indian leaders in the course of their attack on the Bombay Land Revenue Amendment Bill, whose provisions regarding the right of the government to resettle forfeited lands on such terms and for such periods as it might consider fit were declared by them to be intended not only to take away the ryot's power of alienation but also his perpetual tenure or right of permanent and inheritable occupancy and were, thus, regarded by them as a covert attempt at implementing the theory of state landlordism and at virtual nationalisation of land.146 Some of them predicted that the real consequences of these 'revolutionary' provisions would be the very opposite of those intended, in so far as the ryot, in order to save his right of full proprietorship fromn forfeiture, would become even more dependent on the moneylender, who, in turn, would try to foreclose and take possession of the ryot's land in order to forestall its forfeiture by the government.147 A few of them questioned the wisdom of the attempt to maintain intact an inefficient and impoverished peasantry, since this would invariably lead to the stagnation of agriculture. On the other hand, they argued, free transfer of land resulted in its passing into the hands of more efficient and resourceful peasants and capitalists who could invest capital and raise agricultural technique and production to a higher level.148 The clarity with which this point of view was advanced in iS8o by Justice Ranade, its chief exponent, is brought out in a separate section below.

Only rarely did the Indian leaders offer injury to the moneylender as a ground for opposing the imposition of restrictions. on the transfer of land.149 Rather, they were at pains to refute that they had any pro-moneylender proclivities. For example, both Pherozeshah Mehta and G. K. Gokhale vehemently denied that their opposition to the Boinbay Land Revenue Amendment Bill was in the least inspired by a regard for the moneylender's interest.150

On a wider plane, the Indian leaders felt that to restrict alienation of land was to attack the problem of rural indebtedness at the wrong end, the entire scheme being based on ‘a grievous misapprehension of the fundamental conditions of the problem'. True, the right to transfer land enabled a ryot to borrow by offering his land as security, but, they argued, it was not the cause of his borrowing. They therefore contended that in order to check the evil of large-scale land transfers it was not the credit of the ryot but the factors responsible for his borrowing which should be attacked. To check the former while letting the latter remain was ‘only suppressing the symptom while maintaining the original cause of the disease'.151 In this context, they repeatedly controverted the view that the Indian peasant was by nature improvident and would, therefore, always go on borrowing to the maximum limit of his capacity.15z They also poured ridicule over the view that the lightness of the government demand on land indirectly led, by creating an unearned surplus, to the moneylender's control over land.153 They complained that while the government was willing to clutch at any straw it was unwilling to remove the one real cause of indebtedness, namely, the high pitch and rigidity of land assessment, simply because its own financial interests would be affected thereby.154 Remarkable insight in this respect was revealed by Satis Chandra Mukherjea in an article on 'The Economic Situation in India' that appeared in 1899 in the December issue of the Dawn magazine. Tracing the origins of the right of alienation of land in India under British rule, he pointed out that having introduced 'its stringent fiscal system' the Government of India had to take the supplementary step of giving the ryot the right to alienate his land by way of sale or mortgage, otherwise the prompt collection of land revenue would not have been possible. Any amendment of the right of alienation so as to prevent social disruption had, therefore, to be accompanied by substantial modification or even repeal of the fiscal system of which the right of alienation was a necessary corollary.155

Most of all, the Indian leaders were convinced that in the matter of non-alienation legislation the actual effectiveness of the means adopted was not commensurate with the end in view. Since such measures touched only the superficial aspects of the problem of rural indebtedness, they could at the most possess the efficacy of mere palliatives and would leave the real problem untouched and unsolved.156

## VII. ALTERNATIVE REMEDIES

The basic approach of the Indian leaders towards the problem of rural indebtedness was that emphasis should be placed not so much on anti-moneylender measures as on the more positive steps which would remove the causes that compelled the ryot to go to the usurious and grasping moneylender and which would replace the latter by better agencies of rural credit. This view was succinctly put forward by the Mahratta of 8 Octo. ber 1899 as follows:

But so long as want will continue to create a craving for borrowing money, the agriculturist and the money-lender will put their heads together and hatch out a plan for evading the wholesome object of these Land Alienation Acts... Government must do what it can, either to lessen the burden of assessment and thus reduce, in part at least, the cause of indebtedness or be themselves money-lenders and thus meet the proper wants of agriculturists, saving them from the clutches of unscrupulous and heartless private money-lenders.157

Similarly, G. K. Gokhale, in the course of his speech on the Bombay Land Revenue Amendment Bill, challenged the government to select a small area, take over the debts of the ryots from the sahukars there, start agricultural banks to provide for their ordinary needs, and then declare their lands inalienable. 'This would be facing the question in the only manner in which it ought to be faced', he asserted, and went on to declare that 'many of our countrymen will support Government in such a policy'. The heart of the matter, he felt, was that the ryot could not be given any relief unless his existing debt was reduced and his necessary needs of credit were duly met; it was 'not possible to improve the position of the agriculturist by a mere manipulation of the legislative machine'.158 In short, the palliatory remedies must be accompanied by more fundamental and permanent remedies which would try to get rid cf the disease itself.159

Besides suggesting the eradication of rural poverty by ‘opening out of new avenues for labour and industry and surplus capital'i6o and the relaxation of the land revenue demandi61 as remedial measures, the Indian leaders laid considerable emphasis on the provision of easier and cheaper credit facilities to the ryot. They argued in this connection that, while it was necessary to save the ryot from the rapacity of the moneylender, it was equally necessary to provide him with facilities for borrowing the necessary funds at low rates of interest; to stop the functioning of the moneylender without providing alternative sources of credit would be tantamount to leaving the ryot at the mercy of the sahukar but on much worse conditions than before. They therefore agitated throughout the period under study for the starting of a network of agricultural banks in the country.162 And when in 1903-04 the Government of India brought forward the Co-operative Credit Societies Bill to enable it to promote agricultural banks and credit societies they offered the Bill a full-throated welcome.163 An important aspect of the nationalist attitude in the context was their firm belief that the government must take an active part in promoting agricultural banks and not just leave the work to private effort.164 Justice Ranade, G.V. Joshi, and G. K. Gokhale also maintained that the old debts of the debt-ridden peasants must be compounded and liquidated before they could be expected to stand on their own feet with the aid of agricultural banks.165

## VIII. CAPITALIST FARMING

While the ways and means of protecting the ryot from the excesses of landlords and moneylenders suggested by the government and the national leadership in general would improve his condition within the existing structure of land relations, Justice Ranade, basing himself upon the experience of land legislation in Prussia, Russia, and France, advanced the policy of placing the Indian land relations on an entirely new footing.i66 While he was in full sympathy with the ryot and supported various official efforts, and put forward his own proposals, to save the ryot from the oppression of the state, the landlord, and the moneylender, he did not agree with the direction and the spirit of the official measures which, he believed, did not remove the existing bondage of, and the restrictions on, the agrarian classes, as the Prussian legislation had successfully done during the first half of the 19th century. Under the Indian tenancy legislation, he pointed out, both the landlord and the ryot would continue to live within the bonds of the old pattern of agrarian relations; all that such legislation would succeed in accomplishing would be to increase 'the existing complexity of rights and interests', to reduce the landlords to 'the class of Rent-receiving Pensioners', and teach the ryots ‘to look more and more to the State as their sole Landlord, without acquiring the training necessary to raise them to a sense of their position'. All this amounted to 'tinkering'with existing rights in the assumed interests of one class', while what was called for was 'an urgent Reform, and a radical Reform'. The result of such tinkering would be that 'the interests and ownership in land will still continue to be as divided as before between quarrelsome partners, and no real improvement will take pace'.167 Similarly, objecting to proposals for making land nou-alienable with a view to save the ryot from expropriation bv the moneylender, Ranade opined: 'The forcible prohibition of all transfer of real property, voluntary and involuntary, can never mend matters—-it can only stereotype existing poverty and aggravate existing helplessness.'168

In place of the existing land relations, Ranade called for the establishment of land relations based on ‘individual and independent property';169 in place of a protected tenant, he called for the bringing into existence of a free and independent ryot who would be unencumbered in' every way, who would enjoy his property in absolute possession, and who would therefore work hard on his land under the inspiration of 'the magic of property'.17o At the same time, he believed that agriculture based entirely on small peasants would, in Indian conditions, neither be stable and progressive nor utilise the best energies of all classes of people nor make proper use of public works, advanced techniques, etc. 'A complete divorce from land of those who cultivate it is a national evil, and no less an evil is it to find one dead level of small Farmers all over the land', he wrote.i7ı For a proper and balanced development of agriculture, it was necessary to have, he asserted, a large class of capitalist farmers, who would be, unlike the zamindars, complete owners of their land on the model of British landlords or the German junkers. As early as 1879 he had expressed the hope that, once the land was free of artificial restrictions, 'the provident and thrifty classes will succeed to the ownership of land, and a class of landlords would spring up all over the country, whose interest it will be to make the most of the resources of the soil, and of the great public works constructed by the Government.'i72 A little further he had made the interesting remarks :

In all old and backward countries like India, there is always only a minority of people who monopolise all the elements of strength. They are socially and religiously in the front ranks, they possess intelligence, wealth, thrifty habits, knowledge, and power of combination. The majority of the population are unlettered, improvident, ignorant, disunited, thriftless, and poor in means. No political manipulation can hold the balance between these two classes, power must gravitate where there is intelligence and wealth, and it is a hopeless struggle to keep up a poverty-stricken peasantry in possession of the soil, and divorce the natural union of capital and land.173

During his examination of the Bengal Tenancy Bill of 1883, Ranade criticised it for tending to reduce Khamar land, or land in personal possession of the zamindars, and to increase land under the tenants.174 Contrasting the provisions of this Bill with those of the Prussian land legislation, he praised the latter for permitting the old feudal lords to convert a part of their estates into large capitalist farms under their absolute, unencumbered possession.175 Similarly, among other reasons for which he criticised the land revenue policy of the government an important one was his belief that it hampered the growth of large-scale capitalist agriculture.176 In the same way, he was opposed to the imposition of restrictions on the right to transfer land mainly because of the fear that such restrictions would check the inevitable tendency towards ‘concentration of the landed capital of the country'.177

Ranade therefore·advocated that the future development of agrarian relations in India should be based on the creation of two basic agrarian classes which would live side by side: (a) a large petty peasantry which would be free of all encumberances, whether of the state or the landlords, and which would be bolstered by a permanent and low land tax and the provision of cheap credit through agricultural banks; and (b) a large class of capitalist farmers and landlords who, being unhampered by any tenancy rights, etc., would be in complete possession ot their land and in a position to invest capital and utilise the latest advanced techniques of. agriculture. In this 'respect he held up for approval and imitation the pattern of land ownership, based on the growth of absolute property both in the landlord class and in the peasantry, that had come into existence in Prussia by the middle of the 19th century.178 In Prussia in 186o, he pointed out, 15 per cent of culturable land belonged to the state or the church, 44 per cent to large estate owners, 35 per cent to peasant proprietors, and 5 per cent to petty owners. Thus, he approvingly remarked, land had been divided equally 'between the rich landlords and the poor Free men', and the feudal serf had been converted into a free proprietor and the feudal lord into an unencumbered owner of his property.179 Applying the lessons of Prussia to India, Ranade came to the conclusion that

If this country sadly wants a proud and independent Yeomanry as the backbone of its strength and prosperity, it no less equally needs the leading and the light of Propertied men.... High and petty farming, with an upper ten thousand of the holders of large landed estates, and a vast mass of peasant farmers, this mixed constitution of rural society is necessary to secure the stability and progress of the Country.180

Ranade also discussed the ways and means of bringing into existence the class of capitalist farmers and landlords. In this connection, he surmised that some of the thrifty independent ryots would slowly extend their position and rise 'to a better position.181 He also hoped that many people from the moneyed classes would be attracted towards land and would buy the lands of the ‘indifferent and lazy' ryots and thus come to perform the function of capitalist landlords.182 This remedy would obviously be applicable to the ryotwari areas. For the zamindari areas of Bengal, Ranade suggested the copying of the Prussian land reforms. First of all he advised that all agrarian reforms in the zamindari areas should take place 'without any violent disruption of the economical relations of different Classes of Society, and without the shock of Revolution or internecine class struggles'.183 One of the grounds on which he opposed the Bengal Tenancy Bill was that, as its inevitable consequence, 'class will be set against class'.184 He was also opposed to any one-sided expropriation of the existing rights of the zamindars as, in his opinion, the Bengal Bill tended to do.185 Moreover, he believed thiat abolition of any of those rights should be duly compensated.i86 Ranade's own scheme was in favour of harmonising the interests of the zamindars and ryots by raising both to the status of independent proprietors of the soil. He proposed that full proprietary rights should be conferred on the ryots but only over a part of the land under their tenancy and that, as the price of purchase of these rights, the ryots should resign all rights over the remaining part of the land in favour of the zamindars. Thus one part of the land under tenancy would be assigned in full ownership to the ryot and the other part, also in full ownership, to the landlord. If a balance was still due to the zamindar, he might be compensated over a certain number of years by means of payment of money rent charges.187 In short, the zamindars should be paid compensation for the loss of zamindari rights and permitted to resume a part of the land under tenancy for personal cultivation. Under this scheme the absentee rent-receiving zamindars would be transformed into entrepreneurial capitalist farmers and landlords. Ranade also tried to give a more concrete shape to these proposals by proposing that in Bengal ½rd of the occupancy land and ½ of the non-occupancy land should be made over to the ryot in absolute right and i/3rd of the occupancy land and ½ of the non-occupancy land should be handed over as personal land to the landlord. Since he believed that this division would leave the zamindar partially uncompensated, he further proposed that to redress the balance of rights and liabilities the ryot should continue to pay his old rent on the reduced holding for a period of thirty or forty years, 'so as to repay balance of purchase money with interest'. The government might help by advancing the money to the ryots to purchase outright their landlord's claims which, according to Ranade, would amount to about Rs. 34o crores.188 Thus, Ranade hoped to ‘achieve the liberation of the Ryots in Bengal in a generation or two without any violence to vested rights'.189 It may be noted that the entire scheme of Ranade for the creation of independent peasants and capitalist landlords in Bengal was biased in favour of the zamindars, and that the land reforms he proposed were oriented not so much towards the creation of independent farmers as towards the transformuation of the rent-receiving zamindars into capitalist landlords. Independent property in land was to be created not by distribution of land to the ryots, but by handing it over to the landlords. As a matter of fact, his plan of land reforms was so unfavourable to the tenants that it was unimaginable that they would prefer to exchange their status as tenants, protected or unprotected, for the new title of independent owners of land.

While vague support to Ranade's seminal plan for the institutional reorganisation of Indian agriculture was given by some of the Indian leaders when they asked for the union of capital and labour to develop the agriculture of the country,19o more specific and detailed support was rather rare. One such occasion was the speech delivered by Deorao Vinayak at the Congress session of 189o wherein he said that land should be held mainly by those who could afford to improve it and not by poor hand-to-mouth cultivators'. He claimed that agricultural wealth could be best increased 'by the creation of a class of small capitalists—a middle class in fact'.191 As opposed to this support, Satis Chandra Mukherjea, editor of the Dawn, specifically opposed capitalist farming on the ground that it created unemployment and led to loss of self-respect on the part of the peasant.191a

## IX. AGRICULTURE AND INDUSTRY

Some of the Indian leaders viewed the problem of development of agriculture and improvement of the economic condition of the peasant in the wider context of the Indian economy and arrived at the conclusion that progress in agriculture was vitally linked with the simultaneous and rapid industrial development of the country, and that unless the tendency towards the ruralisation of the country was reversed radically no amount of 'tinkering' with the agrarian problem would serve a really useful purpose.

As the initiator of this point of view, Ranade argued, as early as 1881, that, since the malady from which Indian agriculture suffered lay ‘deep in the heart of the political system', ‘no mere external alleviation or dressing of the surface skin will prove of any avail'. What was needed, more than anything else, to remedy the situation was a diminution of the state burden on agriculture and a reduction of 'the pressure of the agricultural population on the land by the opening out of new avenues for labour and industry and surplus capital'.192 Later, in 1892, in his address on 'Indian Political Economy', Ranade propounded the view that a country consigned for ever to dependence on agriculture would be condemned to remain poor and grow still poorer since agriculture had to work ‘under the bane of the Law of Diminishing Returns' and, in India, 'under the disadvantage of an uncertain rainfall'.193

The close inter-dependence between agricultural and industrial progress was repeatedly stressed in a series of editorials by the Mahratta during 1881-84. On 4 September 1881, for example, it opixed:

There is a superfluity of agricultural labour in the agricultural labour market, and unless that is removed from it and employed elsewhere, no remedial measure to improve the wretched condition of the agriculturist will be productive of permanent good results. The development of agriculture and mechanical industry must be simultaneous.

Similarly, in its issue dated 12 February 1882 it argued that 'no mere legislation, no banks, not even the permanency of tenure would go to improve the condition of the peasantry without the introduction of several industries', since no merely agricultural country could ever prosper.194 This argument was echoed by the Native Opinion of 25 May 1884.

In 1895, P. C. Ray endorsed Ranade's view and declared that 'a nation of agriculturists will always compare most unfavourably with a nation of artisans and manufacturers'. The revival of the dying Indian industries and the introduction of the modern industries from the West so as to 'relieve agricuiture of its redundant labourers' would be the best solution of the country's economic difficulties.195

G. V. Joshi blamed the over-crowding of agriculture for the unhealthy and excessive competition for land, which in turn forced up the rents to ruinous heights, led to sub-division of land, and weakened the ryot's motive to improve land.196 Moreover, the over-crowding of agriculture resulted in the underemployment and enforced idleness of millions, or, in other words, in ‘a ruinous waste of economic energy'. According to his calculations, more than half the rural population was in fact without proper work.197 He, too, therefore recommended that non-agricultural industries should be developed 'to draw off the superfluous numbers'. 'Thie more the Ryot is relieved from the existing unhealthy and abnormal pressure by the opening up of such new outlets for labour, the freer the conditions of his work would be and the greater his chances of success', he maintained.i98 He also emphasised that so long as India remained dependent on the single industry of agriculture, 'the root of our economic difficulties will remain untouched'.199 Tilak's Kesari affirmed both these contentions in its issues dated 18 June 1901 and 11 November 1902.200

G. Subramaniya Iyer also gave unequivocal expression to this line of thinking in his book, Some Economic Aspects of British Rule in India, published in 1903. Expressing his agreement with the Finance Minister of Russia, M. De Witte, that so long as a country remained a purely agricultural country it would not be able to ‘get rid of periodic famines and general destitution', he alleged that in this respect India was even in worse position than Russia, since eight-tenths of its population was dependent upon agriculture and the rest 'mainly on cooly work or trivial unproductive occupations'. 'Until this serious anomaly in the economic condition of India is redressed', he wrote, 'the millennium will not arrive either to the peasant or to other classes. The present dead level of poverty will not be removed.' Industrialisation of the country was, therefore, 'the essential condition of India's prosperity'.201

# CHAPTER XI

# Public Finance, I

There is no country possessing a civilised administration where taxation is so light.

— JOHN STRACHEY

There are, however, some things which are yet to be taxed, so that the triumph of the English may be complete. Among such things may be mentioned the skin of the Indian people and their atmosphere.

-The Kesari, 31 January 1888

In every country administered by a just Government it will be considered proper to impose taxes on the rich to a greater extent than on the poor, for only the rich are benefited by the organisation of the police, the establishment of judicial courts, the railways, the telegraph, etc. But if the Government of a country is in the hands of the upper classes, will they be so foolish as to impose taxes upon themselves?

-The Swadesamitran, 18 February 1888

Next to the industrial and tariff policies of the Government of India and the problem of the 'drain', it was its financial policy that drew the severest fire from the Indian national leadership during the period under study. The determining features of Indian finance in this period were: the large increase in military expenditure as a result of the accentuation of Anglo-Russian rivalry in Asia and the consequent fear of a Russian invasion of India, the fall in the gold value of silver, rapid expansion of railway expenditure, and increasing taxation necessitated by the growth of expenditure as a result of all these factors. Even though in some of the years Indian Finance Members were able to show surplus budgets, as a whole these were years of financial disequilibrium and embarrassment and of taxes chasing and trying to keep abreast of expenditure and often failing in the effort.1 Surprisingly enough, however, even though year after year individual Fnance Members bewailed their' hard lot, a very sanguine view of India's finances was taken by British and British Indian statesmen whenever they discusşed the topic in general. Thus, in 1893, in his book the Indian Polity, General Chesney went into raptures over 'the remarkable stability of Indian finance'.2 After reviewing the Indian finances between 1878-98 James Westland boldly asserted in the Financial Statement for 1898-9 that 'the record of twenty years' finance which I have displayed in the above figures is a better one than can be shown by almost any country in the world but the United Kingdom'.3 In 19o1, George Hamilton, Secretary of State, expressed his sense of wonder and pleasure at the 'stability and recuperative power in the financial system of India'.4

The national leadership, on the other hand, took just the contrary view. For example, the Amrita Bazar Patrika of 3o March 1882 declared the financial system of India to be ‘unsound from the top to the bottom'. At the National Congress cf 1894, D. E. Wacha contended that it was bad finance which was ‘at the root of our complaint', and that ‘if India is lost to the British it will be on account of its miserable finances'.s In his Presidential Address to the Congress of 1895, Surendranath Banerjea, after quoting John Bright to the effect that the condition of the people of any country could be adjudged by examining its financial condition, declared that judged by this criterion India's condition was ‘truly deplorable' since the financial position of India was ‘one of ever-recurring defict, and of ever-increasing debt'.6 In his essay on ‘The Present Financial Position', published in January 1896, G. V. Joshi firmly refuted the official contention that Indian finances represented a brilliant feat of financial skill and maintained that the country stood 'on the brink of a financial crisis of unprecedented magnitude and severity'.7 The dangers of the situation were, moreover, aggravated by the fact that the existing state of affairs was not the result of the actions and mistakes of 'this or that individual administrator'; it was 'the system—the entire financial management of the countrywhich is responsible for the present difficulties'.8 Similarly, G.K. Gokhale in his very first speech in 19o2 on the Budget in the Imperial Legislative Council drew a harrowing picture of India's finances since 1885-6 and alleged that since that year ‘our finances have been so managed as to lend support to the view that other interests take precedence of Indian interests in the administration of Indian revenues'.9

Starting with this critical view of the nature and management of India's finances, the Indian leaders examined at length the official fiscal policy and suggested alternative policies. In particular, they took up for examination, condemnation, and popular agitation every important act of the government that they thought to be an instance of the imposition of new taxes on the masses, or of unnecessary, wasteful, or non-welfare expenditure, or of 'injustice in the financial relations between India and Britain. And from each such instance they sought to draw appropriate conclusions regarding the nature and motivations of British rule in India.

## I. TAXATION

The gross revenue of the Government of India increased from Rs. 74.3 crores in 188o to Rs. 90.6 crores in 1893 and Rs. 172.2 crores in 19o4.1o But these figures contain an element of distortion in so far as they include, amóng other things, the gross railway receipts of state railways and irrigation projects and thé net railway receipts on account of other railways. Moreover, certain other factors like changes in the unit of account also undermined the comparability of financial statistics from year to year.11

Opposite opinions were held by the British authorities and the national leaders regarding the burden of this revenue on the people. A preliminary objection was taken by most of the British writers and officials to equating the whole of government revenue with taxation.12 Many of them, for example, tended to regard land revenue as state rent and not a tax.13 Similarly, they denied that opium revenue constituted a tax on the people of India.14 The Indian writers, on the other hand, classified nearly all the important items of government revenue as taxation. Dadabhai Naoroji, for instance, defined taxation as 'the amount which the government of the country takes for its administration, public debt, etc.' from the total annual income of the country.15 In view of the present accepted connotation of the term, the definition given by the Indian leaders may be taken as more correct

The British administrators invariably asserted that India was very lightly taxed and that most probably it was the most lightly-taxed country in the world. For instance, in 1894 John Strachey, ex-Finance Member, confidently proclaimed, 'There is no country possessing a civilised administration where taxation is so light' as in India.i6 Curzon was no less sure of this position. Refuting G. K. Gokhale's assertion that taxation presses heavily upon the people of India, he sardonically remarked in 19o2: 'If the Hon'ble Member were to transfer his residence to any European country, I expect that he would very soon be back again here, with altered views about fiscal matters.'17

The British administrators stressed in this respect the low incidence in sheer physical terms of taxation per head in India as compared to other countries and particularly in Britain. As early as 186o, James Wilson, the then Finance Member, had contrasted'the tax burden of 5s. per head in India with that of £ 2. 3s. per head in England.18 Eleven years later, the Viceroy. Lord Mayo, tried to prove the light character of taxation in India by stating that it amounted to only is. iod. per head.19 He, of course, deducted the land revenue, opium revenue, tributes and such other receipts from the total gross revenue of the government for arriving at this figure. Working on the same assumption, Henry Fowler, Secretary of State for India, asserted in 1894 that 'the taxation per head in India is something like 2s. 6d., or 1/zoth the taxation of the United Kingdom and 1/13th of that of Ireland.'2o

Interestingly enough, the increase in the revenues of India during the period 188o to 1904—which, it was claimed, occurred without additional measures of taxation—was cited by the British Indian officials as a proof of the growing prosperity of the country.21 'The increase in the different branches of revenue indicates', asserted General Chesney, ‘a gradual and steady, but moderate, advance from year to year in the general prosperity of the country.'2z 'We have shown', proudly remarked Curzon in 1903, 'steadily improving revenues, large and increasing surpluses, advances in all the tests that indicate material prosperity.'3 Some of the British officials further maintained that the burden of taxation as such had actually been going down since 1860.24

The Indian national leaders completely rejected these official contentions.25 They emphatically asserted that taxation in India was a crushing and unbearable burden beyond the capacity and resources of the country and that it had reached its uttermost limits. The oppressiveness of the burden of taxation was, as a matter of fact, one of the regular iteins in the nationalist charge-sheet against British rule, and it moved many an Indian leader to express himself on the subject in strong, bitter and angry language.

As early as 1838, we find the Young Bengal complaining, among other things, against 'the enormous taxation to which the country is subjected'.26 In 1871, the Bombay Association submitted a petition to the House of Commons alleging that 'over-taxation has, for many years of British Rule, been the bane of India'.27 In 1879, Surendranath Banerjea contended that it must be ‘apparent even to the most cursory observer that the furthest limit of taxation has been reached in this country and that any further attempt to levy fresh burden might be the straw which would break the camel's back'.28 Similarly, Ranade remarked in 188o that 'further increase of taxation must be adjured as political insanity'.29 Opining that there was 'a limit beyond which it is impossible to push taxation' and that there were 'many evidences to show that India has reached that limit', the Amrita Bazar Patrika gave the following warning in its issue of 27 August 1885: 'When thoughtless rulers push beyond that limit they bring about a rebellion, or if the people are too weak for that, nature herself intervenes and gives unmistable evidence of her wrath. That the limit has been over-stepped in India is evidenced by the fact of successive famines.' Even at the first Indian National Congress taxation was the subject of many a bitter comment. Dadabhai Naoroji accused the rulers of 'screwing out more and more taxes, like squeezing a squeezed orange—inflicting suffering and distress'.3º Besides, Tilak's Kesari remarked in its issue of 31 January 1888 that 'No article in India has escaped taxation. Even the foliage of trees has been taxed'. Waxing sarcastic, the Kesari suggested: 'There are, however, some things which are yet to be taxed, so that the triumph of the English may be complete. Among such things may be mentioned the skin of the Indian people and their atmosphere.'31 The Satya Mitra of 9 February 1888. warned that the government must 'fully bear in mind that even the meek cow can, if enraged, tear the elephant to pieces or will kick the milkman if he attempts to milk after she has yielded the whole of the milk in her udder'. And in an almost seditious tone, it threatened: 'The queen of Jhansi, Nizam Ul-mulk, Chimnaji Appa, Bajirao, Tatya Topi and other heroic personages are, though in history, still brandishing their swords before the world and giving evidence of our heroism.'32 R. M. Sayani, the Congress President for the year 1896, was equally convinced that ‘the ability of the country to bear any fresh taxation is exhausted'.33 Similar sentiments and opinions were expressed by many other nationalist leaders over the years.34

The Indian leaders further complained that not only was taxation in India high but it was constantly on the increase.35. For example G. V. Joshi calculated in 1896 that the burden of taxation proper, even after excluding Railway and other commercial receipts, had increased by 35 per cent during the period 1883-4 and 1895-6.36

To prove their. contention that the burden of taxation in India was very heavy, the Indian leaders, like their official adversaries, appealed 'to the testimony of· figures'. But this testimony they interpreted in their own way. They did accept that the absolute amount of taxation per head was very small. But, they argued, the incidence of taxation was not to be judged from absolute figures of what was taken from a person irrespective of his income. Here not the amount ot taxation per head but the proportion it bore to per capita income was significant. If in India taxation was low, the national income was lower still. Seen in this light, the Indian leaders said, taxation in India turned out to be very heavy and oppressive indeed. Dadabhai Naoroji was the chief architect of this line of argument, which he evolved as early as 1871 in his statement to the Select Committee on East India Finance. He contended that the incidence of taxation was about 8 per cent in England and about 16 per cent in India.37 He, as well as other nationalist leaders, repeated this figurative representation of their case over the years, though they often varied the percentages.38

Some of the Indian leaders further felt that even this ratio of taxation to income failed to bring out properly the real heaviness of the burden. Taxation, they said, must be further correlated to the over-all capacity of the people to bear the taxes. It was one thing to take a certain percentage of income as tax from a poor person and quite another to take the same percentage from a rich one for the tax would definitely impinge with much greater harshness on the standard of living of the former. This line of reasoning found a clear and distinct enunciation in the writings of Dadabhai Naoroji, who used it to resolve the apparent paradox in the nationalist position, viz., the admittedly low per capita taxation crushing the Indian people. He elucidated this point in his statement to the Select Committee on East India Finance in 1871:

It must, moreover, be particularly borne in mind that, while a ton may not be any burden to an elephant, a few pounds may crush a child;...The capacity to bear a burden with ease, or to be crushed by it, is not to be measured by the percentage of taxation, but by the abundance, or otherwise, of the means or income to pay it from. From abundance you may give a large percentage with ease; from sufficiency, the same burden may be just bearable, or some diminution may make it so; but from insufficiency, any burden is so much privation.39

He repeated this formulation in his paper on the 'Poverty of

India' and later on expounded it in numerous speeches, etc.4o R. M. Sayani, President of the Indian National Congress in 1896, echoed Dadabhai's reasoning in very clear terms in his Presidential Address.41

This line of thought, when applied to India, led Dadabhai to declare that not only was the pressure of taxation in India double that of England but it was in fact far more oppressive, as it was exacted from poverty'.42 Similarly R. M. Sayani concluded that 'the ratio of 16½ per cent, of taxation to income, in the case of India, though nominally only two-and-a-half times higher than the ratio in the case of England, is, in incidence, considerably more heavy, and India is therefore, in reality, worse off than the ratio indicates'.43 K. T. Telang, Madan Mohan Malaviya, G. K. Gokhale, R. C. Dutt and several other leaders also correlated in a similar fashion the poverty of the country and the burden of taxation.44 In 1896, G. V. Joshi laid down the perfectly sound doctrine that no increase of taxation should take place unless the total national product was growing, and claimed that in India the former was increasing while the latter was declining.45 Dadabhai Naoroji and Madan Mohan Malaviya put the same proposition in slightly different words when they repeatedly asserted that the Indians would gladly pay higher taxes if only these followed increasing national income.46

The Indian leaders also cited as a pragmatic demonstration of their contentions an array of quotations from English :authorities to the effect that there was no margin of taxation left for the government to fall back upon in the hour of necessity and that India could not be taxed without bringing about an economic and political disaster.47

Later, when budgetary surpluses began to appear, they were advanced by the nationalists as yet another proof of the fact that 'ore taxes are wrung from the people than are right, necessary or advisable'.48 G. K. Gokhale, who made an analysis of the recurring surpluses the central theme of his budget speech in 19o2, started his onslaught on the financial policy of the Government of India by declaring that these surpluses illustrated ‘in a painfully clear manner the utter absence of a due correspondence between the condition of the people and the condition of the finances of the country'. It was obvious that 'the weight of public taxation has been fixed and maintained at an unjustifiably high level'; and thus 'the apparent paradox of a suffering country and an overflowing treasury stands easily explained'.49

Some of' the economists among the national leaders also looked into the question of the distribution of the burden of taxation among the various classes of people. A detailed inquiry into the incidence of taxation conducted by the Finance Department of the Government of India in early 188o's had shown 'most conclusively' that the major burden of taxation in India fell mainly upon the poorer classes.5o The nationalist economists concurred with this view and strongly criticised the system of taxation for its regressive character, that is, for its tendency to bear more heavily upon the poor than upon the rich. As early as 1871, Dadabhai Naoroji took the government to task for exacting more from the cultivators than from other classes of people and sarcastically enquired: 'Or is it that, because richer interests can resort to agitation, and make themselves heard, while the poor labourer and cultivator cannot, it is felt easier to squeeze them than the other classes?'si G. V. Joshi discussed the question at length in April 1888 in his essay, 'The Burmah Deficit and the Enhancement of the Salt Duties'. Condemning the Finance Member for following the policy of taxing the poor 'while letting off rich Zamindars and his rich friends, the tea-planters,'s2 Joshi bemoaned the inequity of a system of taxation under which 'the richer few, who profited most by British admninistration, British justice and British peace, paid least, while the poorer millions, who profited least, paid most'. He also pointed out that the inequality of the burden of taxation existed not only as between one class and another but also as between 'the “classes" in the cities and the "masses" in the country'.53 As a guide to future action, he advised the government to accept as its first duty the rectification and removal of the inequalities in the distribution of public taxation.54 Earlier in 1886, he had laid down the following principle of taxation:

And in all financial discussions, the one question for the practical financier is not, whether the total burden of taxation is heavy or light on the general population, — which is a point of practical politics,—but whether, taking the whole body of public burdens, it is equally and equitably distributed;... (and) the weight of it falls on all classes in just and equitable proportion to their ability to pay.ss

Pleading for the enhancement of the income tax in place of an increase in the salt tax, the Swadesamitran, owned and edited by G. Subramaniya Iyer, in its issue dated 18 February 1888, echoed Joshi's thoughts as follows:

In every country administered by a juşt Government it will be considered proper to impose taxes on the rich to a greater extent than on the poor, for only the rich are benefited by the organisation of the police, the establishment of judicial courts, the railways, the telegraph, etc. But if the Ġovernment of a country is in the hands of the upper classes, will they be so foolish as to impose taxes upon themselves?s6

In its next issue, dated 25 Feb. 1888, the Swadesamitran laid down the doctrine that 'that only can be called a just Government which relieves the poorer classes from their distress and derives its revenue by taxing the upper and middle class'.s7 Similarly, the public meeting of the inhabitants of Poona held under the auspices of the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha on 18 March 1888 to protest against the enhancement of salt tax complained in its memorial that the poor in India were already heavily taxed while the higher and more wealthy enjoyed 'comparative exemption and relief'.58 Prithwis Chandra Ray was equally vehement in his criticism of the unequal incidence of taxation and called it 'the greater canker'. After analysing in detail the incidence of salt tax, income tax, and land revenue, he concluded that ‘all the burden of mismanaged finance is ultimately laid on the back of the poor'.59 Later, in his budget speech of 1903, Gokhale also expressed the opinion that any proposed relief in taxation should go to the poorer classes because they contributed to the exchequer 'much more than they should, relatively to their resources'.6o

Interestingly enough, this somewhat radical point of view got a strong backing from J. N. Tata, one of the leading Indian industrialists of the day. In a letter to the London Daily Chronicle in 1895, he wrote:

I have always held the opinion that all taxation in India bears most heavily on the poor and most lightly on the wellto-do classes. Those whose life and property require the greatest protection from Government have the least to pay for it, while those who have nearly nothing to lose, even by a change of Government, have often to forego their meals to pay the dues of Government.61

The wide support that all sections of nationalist opinion gave to .this line of thinking will be further brought out in the examination of the Indian attitude towards the salt tax and the income tax, wherein it is shown that most of the Indian leaders upheld the view that if people had to be taxed it was the rich and not the poor who should be taxed.62

## II. INJURY CAUSED BY TAXATION

In the Indian view over-taxation was one of the major and immediate causes of the extreme poverty of India and even of the oft-recurring famines. The tone of nationalist comment in this regard was set by Dadabhai Naoroji, who, in his 'Memorandum on a Few Statements in the Report of the Indian Famine Commission, 188o', contended that heavy taxation was responsible for the 'starvation and death of millions'.63

This view was reiterated over the years by innumerable nationalist publicists and pressmen of India,64 and was finally adopted by the Indian National Congress as one of its official creeds in 1896, when it declared that the recent famines were due to ‘the great poverty of the people' that had in turu been brought on, among other causes, by 'excessive taxation and over-assessment'.65

What is of even greater interest is that, in addition, some of the Indian leaders—and G. V. Joshi above all—very penetratingly condemned high taxation from the point of view of Indian industry and the Indian entrepreneur. They declared that high taxation hampered the process of capital formation and thus made economic development of the country difficult, if not well-nigh impossible.

It was in 1888 that G. V. Joshi first asserted that heavy taxation by the state ‘greatly interferes with the growth of the wage fund and rise of wages'.66 Later, in 189o, in his long article on the ‘Economic Situation in India', he observed that one of the hardships from which 'the industrial worker' in India suffered was the insufficiency of capital, which in turn arose, inter alia, from the fact that 'the low margin for savings out of our gross earnings is further reduced by heavy taxation'. He calculated that the net national savings (gross production minus necessary expenditure of the people for maintenance) amounted to Rs. 9o crores per year, out of which the government took away Rs. 5o crores as taxes. Thus more than one half of the national annual savings was 'swept into the public treasury to meet, what is for the most part, more or less nonproductive expenditure'. There could obviously be no 'greater economic evil than such a heavy drag upon our industrial progress'.67 These sentiments were echoed by G. K. Gokhale in his first budget speech (19oz). The object 'to be worked for in Indian finance, opined Gokhale, should be 'to keep the level of taxation as low as possible, so as to leave the springs of national industry free play and room for unhampered movement.'68

As a matter of fact, many of the Indian leaders raised their demand for low taxation to the level of a theoretical principle, linking it up with the peculiar economic and political conditions of India. This is very well brought out by the Amrita Bazar Patrika of 7 February 1871 which laid down the simple dictum :

Since the people do not tax themselves and they have no control over the finances of the empire, their cry should be for reduction and not for increase of taxation. A constant and firm demand for reduction is in our humble opinion the only wise policy which a people without a Parliament ought to adopt.

G.V. Joshi emphatically pronounced in 1886 that in India because of 'the peculiar conditions of British rule', it was 'essential to the material and moral progress of the nation that the weight of public burdens should be as light as possible'.69 When after 1898 surpluses began to appear regularly in the Indian budgets, most of the Indian leaders demanded remission of taxation.7o In 19o2 Surendranath Banerjea remarked that when the general taxpayer in a country was 'the starving ryot', the remission of taxation became ‘a matter of paramount obligation on the part of the rulers of the land'.7ı The case for low taxation was lucidly put forth by G.K. Gokhale in his speech on the budget for 1905-06:

In all countries, it is an accepted canon of finance that the weight ot pubiic burdens sncū.d be kept as lignt as possible. ...If this is so in rich European countries, it should be much more so in India, where the revenue is raised from a poor, helpless population, and the larger part is contributed by a broken and exhausted peasantry, and where, owing to the special circumstances of the case, the character of public expenditure is such that a great portion of it has to be spent on objects unconnected or but remotely connected with the. moral and material advancement of the people.7²

## III. SOME FACTORS IN THE INDIAN ATTITUDE

Many factors were responsible for the growing popularity of the idea that the country was overtaxed. According to C.N. Vakil, during 1871-19o1 the incidence of taxation in India ranged between 8 to 9 per cent of the average national income.73 Secondly, over taxation or a high level of taxation does create much more hardship in an economically backward country than in a developing country, since it results in 'the forcible expropriation of peasants, artisans, and in a word, all elements of the lower middle-class'. This phenomenon was fully evident in India during the later half of the 19th century. Thirdly, there was no sentiment of patriotism or social consciousness backing the existing level of taxation. On the contrary, the Indian leaders apprehended, as will be brought out later, that because of the foreign nature of the Government of India any revenue in excess of the minimum requirements of administration would be diverted to Imperial purposes. Fourthly, the Indian leaders objected to the government's method of raising Indian revenues through taxes on land and salt and through an excise duty an liquor. Lastly, they correlated the level of taxation with the manner of its utilisation. After all the Government of India acted rigidly within the narrow orbit of the laissez faire theory of the functions of the state and did not undertake any large-scale development activity in those years..

## IV. SOURCES OF REVENUE

We will now proceed to examine the attitude of the Indian national leaders towards the chief sources of revenue of the Government of India—which at the time were land revenue, salt tax, opium and excise revenue, customs revenue, and income tax.74 The bitter opposition offered by the Indian national leaders to the existing extent of, and any increase in, land revenue, and their vigorous advocacy of raising funds through imposition of import duties on selected products have been discuzsed at length in earlier chapters. In the following pages their stand regarding income tax, salt tax, and opium .and excise revenue has been presented and analysed.

## V. INCOME TAX 75

At the time of the commencement of the period of our study, i.e., in 188o, a modified form of income tax, known as licence tax, was being levied in India. A beginning in this direction had been made in 186o, when James Wilson, the first Finance Member, had imposed a tax on all annual incomes above Rs. 2oo arising out of trade, agriculture, professions and government or non-government service. Frequent changes were made in the tax, which was finally abandoned in 1865, with the announcement that it was to serve as 'the great financial reserve of the country'.76 After that year, taxation of personal incomes was. several times resorted to, abolished, and again taken up, though in varying forms.

In i878, the Governnent of India finally made up its mind to make 'due fiscal provision' to face the by-now regular famines by creating a long-terın Faiine Insurance Fund, ınoney for which was to be raised partly by the inposition of special cesses on land revenue and rent and partly by the levy of a licence tax on the commercial and trading classes and artisans. No central legislation was enacted for the purpose, recourse being had to a series of provincial mneasures. The so-called licence tax was ‘in fact a limited incone-tax assessed on a system of classification according to approximate income'.77 Agricultural, professional, and salaried incomes, as well as income from securities, were exempted from the purview of the tax. The extent and nature of the tax varied from province to province. In North-West Provinces, Oudh, and Madras the taxable minimum was Rs. 2oo, while in Bengal, Bombay, and the Punjab it was Rs. 1oo. Incidence of the tax varied according to the classes into which the assessees were divided, but in no case was it to exceed 2 per cent of the aunual profits of the assessee or Rs. 5oo. An abortive attempt was made by the government in 1879-8o to extend .tlie tax to professional and salaried persons. In 188o, the limit of exemption from the tax was raised to Rs. 5oo. At this tiine the tax yielded over half a million sterling and was paid by 228.447 persous.78

The licence tax was generally opposed by the Indian leaders. at the time of its imposition,79 and they continued to demand its abolition in the later years.8o It should, however, be kept in view that they mainly attacked, not the principle underlying the levy of the licence tax, but the manner of its imposition, which was declared by them to be iniquitous and unfair. First of all, they criticised the tax for 'descending very low in the scale of incomes' and thus falling on a large number of poor persons. In fact, some of them were of the opinion that the tax fell too heavily on the poor while leaving the rich relatively free. For example, the Indu Prakash of 14 January 1878 questioned the fairness of a tax which touched the pockets of the big and influential men, traders and men of business, who were mostly Europeans, so lightly while putting the main burden on the small traders and artisans, who were mostly Indians.81 Similarly, the Indian Spcctator of 23 October 1881 described the tax as a hybrid monster, half coward, half bully, passing by the richest and those most enjoying the benefits of good government and sucking the blood of small traders.82 The nationalist newspapers also pointed out that the poor were further hit by the tendency on the part of the petty officials in charge of the tax assessment and collection towards its maladministration.83 Sowe of the Indian newspapers therefore demauded that the exemption limit of the tax should be raised.84 Interestingly enough, the Bcngalee in its issue dated 17 January 188o vehemently objected to the fixation of a maximun limit of the tax as this would 'only have the effect of treating with exceptional favour those millionaire merchants who least require such favour' and who happened to be Europeans. It should be noted that the Indian newspapers were in this case giving expression to the strong sentiments of the urban poor, which were being ventilated in other forms too.85 In Calcutta, for instance, water carriers (bhistis), farriers (nalbands), and carters went on strike during 1S78-9 in protest against the tax..86 In Snrat, serious riots against high assessment of the tax broke out in April 1878.8

The other ground on which the nationalists attacked the licence tax was that high salaried European and Indian officials and thriving professional men were unjustly exempted from its operation.88 Consequently, they pressed for an extension of the tax to the professional and salaried classes.89 The nationalist criticism of the licence tax was fairly summed up in the Petition adopted at a public meeting of the inhabitants of Poona held on 16 May 188o under the auspices of the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha. The petitioners asserted that the tax 'violated al the principles of political economy taxing the very poor and the famine-stricken ut of all proportion to their means, and exempting the high salaried omcials and other rich classes who could best bear additional taxation'.9o

It should also be noted that two of the leading nationalist newspapers of the day, the Amrita Bazar Patrika and the Hindu, explicitly supported the principle of a direct personal tax and pressed not for the abolition of the licence tax but merely for its transformation into an income tax:9

Having surrendered the customs revenue in 188z, the Government of India was soon compelled by the falling rate of exchange and the rising military expenditure to look for new sources of income. Its gaze naturally fell on the licence tax, which yielded at the time the paltry sum of less than half a crore of rupees. In 1886 it imposed an income tax, which was really 'au enlargement, an extension, and equalisation' of the existing licence tax, at least as far as the lowest classes of income were concerned. The new tax was made applicable to the professional and salaried classes also. The rate of the tax was 4 pies in the rupee for incomes below Rs. 2,ooo a year and 5 pies for incomes above that amount. Incomes of Rs. 5oo a year or less were exempted from the tax. In the case of military officers the exemption limit was Rs. 6,ooo a year. Certain other incomes, like those derived from land—including teaplantations—, salaries, pensions and leave allowances paid in England, profits of shipping companies incorporated in England, interest on securities paid in England, and profits of the railways up to the amount of the guaranteed interest, were also put outside the pale of the tax. In the first year of its operation, i.e. in 1886-7, the income tax was paid by nearly 351,000 persons and it brought in nearly Rs. 1.37 crores. About 30 per cent of this amount was charged on salaries and pensions. In 1902-03, the number of assessees rose to nearly 531,0oo and the amount collected to nearly Rs. 2.1 crores.92

The national leadership was in general divided in its attitude towards the income tax. This division is reflected even more clearly if a long enough span of time is taken into account. In fact, some of the Indian leaders took different and even contradictory attitudes at different times.

The proposal for the levy of an income tax in the 188o's as well as its actual imposition in 1886 were opposed on a wide scale by the nationalist press and leaders.93 This opposition "was not, however, so much on account of the nature of the tax itself as for want of nationalist confidence in any proposals for additional taxation—which was a permanent anathema to the national leadership—and thc need for which, it was believed, was unproved. This was, for example, the manner in which the editor of the Voice of India understood the nationalist position when summarising the stand taken by the Indian newspapers.94 Moreover, prior to 1882 inany of the Indian nationalists were afraid that the imposition of an income tax might be designed to create a budget surplus, which might then be used as an cxcuse to abolish import duties.95 After 1882, when import duties were actually repcaled, many of the Indian leaders opposed the incomc tax on the ground that a better method of raising revenue would be to reimpose the import dutics.96 A few of the Indians also suspected that the revenue derived from the tax would be used by the government to meet the rapidly increasing military expenditure, that was gall and wormwood to the nationa--lists.97 What is of much greater importance, a large number from among the ranks of the opponents of the income tax supported one particular aspect of it, namely, the bringing in of the professional and salaried classes within its purview.98

Having said all this, we should also note that when some of the opponents of income tax declared that direct taxation was not at all suited to India99 or when some of them advocated the extension and enlargement of the salt tax in place of the imposition of the income tax,1oo they clearly revealed that their opposition was inspired not so much by their under. standing of popular weal as by narrow class and selfish interests. In any case, V. N. Mandlik, for one, was frank enough to admit publicly in his speech on the Income Tax Bill of 1886 that 'the syinmetry of the Act is marred by the higher classes being put under an income-tax instead of à licence tax'.ioı

But more significant than this opposition to the income tax is the fact—which has, moreover, been more or less ignored by nearly all the historians of the Indian income tax—that instead of opposing the move for the levy of income tax most of the more powerful organs of nationalist opinion extended some sort of support, often active and vigorous, but also in some cases grudging and lukewarm, to the tax before, at the time of, and after, its imposition.

The Amrita Bazar Patrika actively defended the income tax from the year 187o onwards.1oz Many of the nationalist newspapers lent support to John Strachey's effort in 188o to apply the license tax to salaried and professional incomes.1o3 And when Strachey's measure was withdrawn, as a result of opposition from the Englishmen in India, the Amrita Bazar Patrika was moved to bitter sarcasm. Its issue of 5 March 188o carried the following comment, taking the Indian critics of the measure to task:

Now that the tax upon salaries and professions has been abandoned, even people the most stupid will see how senseless was the outcry raised against the announcement of Sir John Strachey of extending the area of taxation. Asses there must be in everv society, or who would bear washermen's clothes on their backs?...but then they must be employed in carrying washermen's clothes and burdens, and not in leading political associations or editing public prints.

In the later years, a large number of prominent nationalist newspapers, like the Hindu, the Indu Prakash, and the Indian Spectator, asked for the levy of an income tax in place of the existing licence tax.104 Finally, at its first session in 1885, the Indian National Congress passed a resolution urging, among other things, 'the extension of the licence tax to those classes of the community, official and non-official, at present exempted from it... .'1os When the income tax was after all levied in 886, it was supported by the Amrita Bazar Patrika, the Hindu, and the Mahratta, as well as by many other nationalist newspapers.106

After 1886 the income tax gained even greater and wider popularity among the nationalist circles, particularly as it became evident that its abandonment would lead to the imposition of other taxes. This fact also substantiates the view that the earlier opposition of some of the Indian leaders was really inspired by their opposition to any new measure of taxation .and not to the income tax as such. Once it was apparent that new taxes had to be levied, the nationalist leaders as a whole opposed the demand for the repeal of the income tax and preferred it to any other form of taxation. This fact was brought out dramatically at the third session of the Indian National Congress in 1887 when a delegate, V. R. Chakravarti Aiyangar, moved an amendment to Resolution VI to the effect that the income tax should be abolished. Immediately there were cries of 'No, no, withdraw,’ 'We don't want to relieve ourselves of taxation,’ 'We won't have it', 'Sit down', and 'Shut up', and Chakravarti Aiyangar was constrained to withdraw his amendment.1o7 At the next session of the Congress in 1888, Madan Mohan Malaviya declared that the Congress ‘approved and desired the imposition, of the income tax on the rich, or those who could afford to pay it',1o8 In 19o2, while moving Resolution XIII, C. Y. Chintamani forcefully condemned the agitation for the abolition of the tax,o9 and the Congress President for that year, S. N. Banerjea, also expressed his lack of sympathy for the demand.o Among the other publicmen of India, Dadabhai Naoroji condemned, in a. letter dated 26 April 1889 to S. M. Malabari, the Indian members of the Viceroy's Council for voting for a salt duty and against an income tax.111 In his Economic History of India, R. C. Dutt praised the income tax (of 186o) for being ‘just and equitable'.112 In 1888 G. V. Joshi put in a vigorous plea for the extension and enhancement of the income tax in place of the enhancement of the salt tax.113 The Indian Association put forth a similar demand in 1894.114

Among the newspapers, the Anmrita Bazar Patrika carried on with its crusade in favour of the income taxii5 and in its issue of 18 April 1893 demanded an increase in its incidence. The Hindu of 28 December 1887 opined that 'the income tax should be the foremost permanent source of Indian taxation'.116 The Sudharak of 15 December 189o declared that ‘a demand for the abolition of the tax would be unmitigatedly selfish'.117 The Sanjivani of 10 March 1894 pleaded for an increase in the rate of the income tax on the ground that the existing rate practically amounted to an exemption of the rich.118 The Bengalee of 4 November 1902 also objected to any repeal of the tax. The Mahratta of 29 May 1904 wanted the tax to be retained in spite of the budgetary surpluses; its proceeds, it suggested, should be taken on trust and spent on education, especially industrial education. Many other Indian newspapers took, over the years, a similar stand against the repeal of the tax and in some cases in favour of the enhancement of its incidence.119 In particular, at the time of the enhancement of the salt tax in i888, a large number of the newspapers advocated an increase in the income tax instead.12o

As against this overwhelming phalanx of nationalist opinion that arose in favour of the income tax after 1886, only a few Indian newspapers and leaders asked for its repeal after that year. The Som Prakash of 1o November 189o and the Sahachar of 11 February 1891 from Bengal and the Bharat Jiwan of 17 November 189o from the N-W Provinces and Oudh were the only newspapers to have done so, as far as we have been able to ascertain.12i Among the political leaders, G. R. Chitnavis, the Maharaja of Darbhanga, and Asutosh Mukerjea—none of them being a nationalist leader of the front rank—were the only ones to have opposed the tax.122

As regards the manner of the imposition of the income tax, some of the Indian leaders criticised from the very beginning the exemption from tax of zamindars and landlords. As early as 17 January 188o, the Bengalec put forth this view and refuted the assertion that the immunity granted to the zamindars was justified since they were already paying the Road Cess and the Public Works Cess. These cesses, it contended, were in reality being indirectly paid by the ryots. The exemption of zamindars from the purview of the Income Tax Act of 1885 was criticised by the Hindu of 7, 9 and 12 January 1886, the Indian Nation of 25 February, 1886, and the Gujarat Mitra of 10 January 1886.123 G. V. foshi argued at length in 1888 for the extension of the income tax to the zamindars, talukdars and planters.124 The Hindu of 9 January 189o, and the Kaiser-e-Hind of 18 February 1894125 also made a similar plea.

A few of the Indian leaders also commented on the absolute lack of progression in the Indian income tax. Objecting to all incomes above Rs. 2,ooo a year being grouped together for purposes of this tax, the Hindu asserted that 'the principle of graduated taxation holds good in India as well as in any other country'.
