It also opined that 2½ per cent of income was too low a rate of taxation for those drawing high salaries.126 In a memorandum dated 8 March 1894, the Indian Association suggested that ‘instead of the necessaries of life being taxed in an indirect manner, the income tax should be raised in a graduated scale, the higher salaries being assessed at a higher percentage'.127 Several other Indian leaders demanded a well graduated income tax.128

Perhaps it would not be out of place to briefly examine at this stage the grounds on which the large majority of the nationalist leaders came to support the income tax or at least its extension to the salaried and professional classes. The first, and perhaps the most important, of these was the understanding that the income tax was the only important tax through which the Europeans in India, whether bureaucrats, employees of private firms, professional men, or merchants, could bemade to contribute their share of the cost of the governance of India.129 The Amrita Bazar Patrika expressed this sentiment in the following words: 'An income tax in India touched the pockets of 1oo Europeans in a 1oo, while it touched the pocket of one native in a thousand'.13o Some of the Indian newspapers even asserted that to oppose the income tax was to play into the hands of the Europeans.131 The second reason for the nationalist support to the income tax was that of social justice and equity in taxation. This extremely democratic point of view was very distinctly articulated in some cases. For instance, the Bengalec of 17 January 188o asserted that ‘if Government is bent upon imposing new taxes rather than retrench expenditure, it is better—far better—that such new and direct taxes should fall on'those who can bear them than that they should fall on the poor'.13 The Amrita Bazar Patrika of 5 March 188o was even more emphatic: 'The real point at issue is not whether direct taxation is suited to India or not, but whether or not the wealthiest members of our society should be made to bear their fair share of burden. If they should, there is no other way of reaching them but by direct taxation.' Next year, the Patrika once again advocated the levy of income tax and, denying the validity of the' argument that it caused so much harassment and inconvenience that its advantages were negated, it wrote in its issue of 29 December 1881: 'First of all, who:cares for the petty inconveniences of the 2. lacs of rich mén in the country, certainly not. the 2oo.millions who pay the salt tax,.the land revenue, and the stamp revenue?'133 The Hindu of 19 December 1884 also pointed out that the poor in India had no reason to object to the rich being taxed; their complaint, on the other hand, would be against the salt tax, excise duty, stamp duty, etc.134. G. V. Joshi also articulated this view very cogently and clearly in 1888. Having defended the imposition of the income tax in 1886, he went on to point out that the burden imposed on the. 'upper and upper-middle classes' by the Income Tax Act of 1886 had not been ‘in any sense or degree, adequate and sufficient or proportionate to the burdens laid on the poorer classes. On the contrary, it was plain that the new measure of taxation...did not go far enough towards an equitable re-adjustment of public taxation....the fact remained that even after the taxation of 1886, the "classes" paid less, and the "masses" more than their due share.'13s

The supporters of the income tax did not, however, support it in each and every respect. While accepting it in principle, they criticised the manner in which it was being levied, According to them, as well as many of the critics of the tax who decided to submit to it with good grace, the worst feature of the income tax as imposed in 1886 was its low limit of exemption. They wanted this limit to be raised from Rs. 500 to at least Rs. 1,ooo a year. Broadly speaking, the nationalist case was that the income tax fell, because of its low exemption limit, with undue severity on people with low incomes, like the poorly paid servants of the government, petty traders, shop-keepers and artisans, who had little margin of income to spare for paying the tax, especially in view of the prevalence of the joint-family system; and that the unsatistactory administration of the tax led to undue harassment and oppression of people earning puny incomes, most of whom were incapable of resisting the extortions and tyrannies of the petty officials put in charge of administering the tax.

Some of the nationalist newspapers suggested, even before the income tax was levied, the fixing of a high exemption limit as a device to remove the sting out of an income tax.136. The Indian National Congress of 1885 also, while recommending the extension of the licence tax to the salaried and professional classes, suggested the maintenance ‘in the case of all classes of a sufficiently high taxable minimum'.137 At the. time of the imposition of the income tax in 1886, many of the Indian leaders pressed for raising its exemption limit.138 At its third session in 1887 the Indian National Congress passed a resolution urging that the taxable minimum be raised to Rs. 1,o0o.139 In the following years this resolution, described by Madan Mohan Malaviya as 'the respectable poor man's resolution',14o became a hardy annual at the Congress sessions.141 The nationalist press kept up throughout the years a regular barrage of agitation on the issue.142 Indian publicmen raised the question again and again, on the Congress platform, iņ the legislative councils, and in print.143

The justice and validity of the nationalist criticism of the working of the income tax came in time to be fully recognised by the authorities, so much so that in 19o3 the government raised the taxable minimum to Rs. 1,ooo per. year.144 This. move was enthusiastically welcomed by the national leadership145 which, with rare exceptions, ceased demanding any further concessions in connection with the income tax.146

## V. IMPLICATIONS OF THE NATIONALIST APPROACH

It is clear from the above analysis of the nationalist attitude towards the income tax that in this instance the overwhelming majority of the nationalist leaders was able to rise above narrow group interests. Most of them were themselves lawyers and journalists, who had, moreover, close family connections. with the salaried groups aud, yet, a large number of them approved of, and even advocated, the imposition of the income tax. Even of those leaders who opposed the tax as such, a large majority supported, and even demanded, its extension to the professional and salaried classes. Thus, there was the widest possible agreement among the nationalists on the desirability of bringing the 'educated middle-classes' within the purview of some sort of direct taxation. And once the income tax had come into existence, not a single important nationalist voice was raised in favour of its repeal in'the following years.

It should be carefully noted in this context that most of the top leaders of the Indiau National Congress as well as the owners and editors of the more important nationalist newspapers, like the Amrita Bazar Patrika, the Hindu, the Mahratta, the Bengalcc, the Hitavadi, the Sanjivani, the Indu Prakash, the Indian Spectator, and the Swadesamitran, were of certainty liable to be assessed to the income tax and were otherwise escaping the dragnet of direct taxes, or even of most of the indirect ones.147 But they were willing to offer themselves for being taxed and. thus, to suffer this self-injury, so that the foreign officials of the Government of India might also be taxed. This attitude of the Indian nationalist leaders is, on the one hand, a sign of the rising tide of sentiments of nationalism and hostility to the foreign rulers of the land at this time aud, on the other. a proof of the willingness of the nationalist leaders of the day to subordinate their owi personal interests to the general, national weal.148

The only section of the population whose interests in the matter were taken up for advocacy by the national leadership was that consisting of persons with annual incomes below Rs. 1,ooo, or, in other words, of poor shopkeepers and lowpaid government employees. It must be pointed out in this connection that the stand of Indian leadership in favour of raising the exemption limit of the income tax was not only quite correct on the grounds of social justice and equity but was on all counts a legitimate political device for attracting the small shopkeeper and the lower-level government servant to the nationalist fold. Even in this case, however, many ot the Indians fought harder for the reduction of the salt tax and land revenue than for raising the exemption level of the income tax. As a matter of fact, some of the Indian leaders recommended, very consciously and overtly, the above order of priorities in any scheme of grant of relief in taxation.149 It may also be noted that the Indian leadership did not, in fact, mechanically represent in the matter of the income ,tax the interests of the big inerchants and landlords, who were constantly pleading and agitating for the repeal of the tax.150

## VI. SALT TAX

The tax on salt was the second most important source of revenue to the Government of India during the years 188o to 1905. The mode of taxing salt varied from province to province. In Bombay, the tax took the form of aņ excise duty, in

Bengal it was levied chiefly as customs duty on imported salt,151 and in Madras, Northern India, and the Punjab it was included in the price fixed by the government on its own production of salt as a monopoly.

The rates at which the salt tax was levied differed widely from province to province till 1878-79,152 when near uniformity was introduced after the notorious inland customs line was abolished. The salt duties were equalised by revising them downwards in Northern India from Rs. 3 to Rs. 2-8 per maund and in Bengal from Rs. 3-4 to Rs. 2-14 and upwards in Madras and Bombay from Rs. 1-13 to Rs. 2-8. For the government the adjustment proved advantageous, as the salt revenue increased .by nearly £1 million during 1875-77 to 1879-81.153 But the burden of this increase was perhaps not felt by the people to the' full extent of the increase because at the same time the market price of salt was favourably influenced by the conteinporaneous extension of transportation facilities.

Even before 1858, the salt duties had come in for criticism at the hands of prominent Indians.154 Up to 1882, the Indian leaders generally pressed for a reduction, or even for the abolition,·of the salt tax; and since the process of equalisation of the tax begun in 1859 had led to a steep rise in it in the Bombay Presidency, naturally the criticism of the tax, as well as of the increase in it, was most vocal in that province.155 For instance, in· 188o Dadabhai Naoroji. called 'the salt tax a stigma' upon the British name.156

We may also simultaneously take note of the fact that another section, though a minor.'one, of the Indian leadership tended to support the salt' tax.'Naturally enough, this section was to be found mainly in Bengal, where changes in the salt tax had been in the downward direction and where the zamindars had always shown a keen anxiety to defend their own interests at the .cost of the interests of the ryots. Two early representatives of the pro-salt tax lobby in Bengal were Raja Digambar Mitra and Kristodas Pal.157 But much more surprising is the fact that we find the Amrita Bazar Patrika also advocating an increase in the salt tax in place of other taxes like the road cess.158 The reason advanced by its editors for' taking such an unexpected stand was that the salt tax in Bengal was primarily an import tax on salt brought from Liverpool and Cheshire and an increase in the tax was likely to be borne entirely by foreigners.159

In 1882, the government reduced the salt tax, fixing it at Rs. 2 per maund throughout the country except in Burma or in the trans-Indus districts of the Punjab. The nationalist reaction to the step was a mixed one. The Mahratta, several other Bombay papers, and some of the Bengal papers, welcomed it and urged the government to reduce the tax further or even to abolish it.16o On the other hand, the Amrita Bazar Patrika and several other papers from Bengal and Northern India felt that the step was not really called for as the salt tax was never felt to be oppressive by the people and that relief should have, instead, been given to those paying the licence tax.161

Between 1882 and 1888, many nationalist voices were raised against the salt tax. For instance, G. V. Joshì, who can be credited with having pioneered the anti-salt tax agitation, started his campaign against the salt tax during these years. In his paper on 'Ways and Means of Meeting the Additional Armv Expenditure', published in April 1886, he made a detailed analysis of the salt tax and its impact on the life and labour of the people. He protested vehemently against the proposal for enhancing the salt tax, condemned the fatal disposition in some quarters' to look upon it as a financial reserve, and deprecated ‘its being maintained as a part of the permanent taxation of the country'.16z At the very first session of the Indian National Congress in 1885, S. A. Swaminath Iyer and V. S. Pantulu inveighed against any attempt to enhance the salt tax and urged the Congress and the people to raise their voice against any such eventuality.163 Some of the leading nationalist newspapers also pleaded for a reduction in, or :abolition of, the salt tax during these ycars.164 On the other side, several·other newspapers advocated in 1886 the 'enhance: ment of the salt tax in place of the imposition of the income tax.165

By 1888, the finances of the Government of India were once again in the doldrums as a result of the conquest and annexation of Upper Burma, military operations on the North-West Frontier, and the continued fall in exchange, and imposition of fresh taxation once again became imperative. This led James. Westland, the then Finance Member, to enhance the duty on salt by an executive order, dated 19 January 1888, from Rs. 2 per maund to Rs. 2-8.166 In 1902, the gross income from the tax amounted to Rs. 9.1 crores as against Rs. 7.6 crores in 1888.167

The enhancement of the salt tax aroused a powerful wave of dissatisfaction, anger, and protest in India. Many leading nationalist newspapers of the country, including the Mahratta, the Hindu, the Bengalee, and even the Amrita Bazar Patrika, denounced the measure in strong and pungent language.168 Some of the Indian language newspapers protested in a manner that bordered on the seditious. For instance, the Kesari, edited at the time by B. G. Tilak, commented in its issue dated 31 January 1888 :

There are no people so miserable as those of India. If a sinful man is to be punished he should be sent to India.... These thoughts have been suggested by the recent order of Government on the subject of the salt duty....This inhuman action could be taken only by him who was unmindful of the utterly distressful condition of the people of India... The present is the time when such men as hold the opinion that the country conquered by the sword should be preserved also by the sword are in the ascendant.... The cat is naturally meek, but when hard-pressed it is likely to turn to bay and to become irresistible. Such a contingency is possible in the case of the Hindu and it is worth remembering that there is fear of a permanent possession being lost by shrinking from a little burden of taxation on the English people.169

The Bodh Sudhakar of 25 January 1888 warned that 'the present addition is but the thin edge of the wedge and its introduction into the mortal wounds of India is fraught with great danger to contentment and peace among the masses of the people'.17o The Maharashtra Mitra of 8 March 1888, provoked among other things by the enhancement of the salt tax, published a dialogue between a Hindu and an Englishman, where the Hindu likens Lord Dufferin to a 'butcher' and to the protests of the Englishman replies: 'Is not the increase in the salt-tax like sucking the life-blood'.171 Similarly, the Praja Bandhu of Bengal exclaimed in its issue dated 27 January 1888: 'Cursed was the hour when His Excellency Lord Dufferin,set foot on Indian soil.'17²

The increase in the salt tax again incited G. V. Joshi to make a scathing attack on the salt tax policy of the governinent in 1888.173 Finally, the question was taken up by the Indiau National Cougress of 1888 which adopted a resolution putting 'on record its disapproval' of the measure.174 Interestingly enough, however, this resolution was carried by the Congress against the wishes of its own Standing Committee which had earlier rejected it on the ground that it was useless' to ask for the repeal of the enhancement so soon after it had been made.175 It is also, therefore, not surprising that none of the top leaders of the Congress spoke on the resolution that was moved and seconded by two back-benchers from the Ratnagiri district.176

As if to take away from the near-unanimity of the nationalist opinion on the question, a few voices were also raised in defence of the official step.177 It is, however, to be noted that most of the earlier dissidents belonging to the Fourth Estate, like the Amrita Bazar Patrika and the Tribune, were missing from their list, having rejoined at this time the mainstream of nationalist sentiment on the question.

This strong condemnation of the salt tax was continued in later years also and a persistent demand for its reduction was continually raised by the national leadership. The Indian National Congress fully represented the natioualist mnood in this respect and year after year passed resolutions demanding an immediate reduction of this tax.178 Many prominent nationalists followed suit.'79 And G. K. Gokhale, the politicianpupil of G. V. Joshi, made the salt duty question one of his favourite political issues, and carried on his master's work from the political platform and in the legislative chamber.18o Similarly, most of the nationalist newspapers carried on over the years an unbroken agitation against the tax.181 It is of soine interest to note in this context the contents of a letter to the editor from an English merchant named R. D. Rusden published in the Mahratta of 21 July 1889. Though obviously it was the expression of an individual's opinion, its publication - that nearly landed the editor in gaol82-inevitably carried some measure of editorial approval by Tilak. Advising the Indian leaders to carry on an agitation modelled on the anti-corn law agitation, Rusden asked the Indians to tell the government that ‘a large proportion of what is called death by famine is really murder by salt tax; the whole thing is hateful and shameful and scandalous, and we insist upon the sweeping away of the tax; and if you do not repeal it, we will try and make it so ho and disagreeable for you that before long you will have to drop it whether you like it or not.' Anticipating the course of action adopted by Mahatma Gandhi about 40 years later, he counselled the Indian leaders 'to advise your people to avoid the tax by preparing their own salt... and you can encourage them to do this by raising funds for their protection and defence in case of need.

Later, when at the turn of the. century surpluses began to appear in the budgets, most of the Indian leaders agitated for their utilisation, first of all, for reducing the salt tax.183 Addressing the Millowners' Association, D.E. Wacha went to the extent of pleading that the salt tax should be reduced even before the cotton excise duty was removed.184

Ultimately, the government announced in 1903 a reduction of 8 annas per maund in the salt tax. As was to be expected, the announcement was greeted joyously by the Indian leadership; at the same time, however, the demand for a further lowering of the tax was put forward.185 The demand was reiterated in the following months and years.186 And when, in 19o5, the government granted a further relief of 8 annas a máund in the tax, the Indians reacted with another round of congratulations, accompanied by a demand for still further reduction.187

However, while the dominant section of Indian national leadership was campaigning against the salt tax, a small minority, mainly led by the Amrita Bazar. Patrika, expressed itself in favour of relief in the income tax or other taxes rather than in the salt tax.188 As already pointed out earlier, the Amrita Bazar Patrika had for a brief spell of time in 1888 reversed its previous support to the salt tax and opposed the increase in the tax; but, after 1888, it once again reverted to its earlier stand.189

## VII. REASONS FOR NATIONALIST ATTACK ON SALT TAX

What were the economic reasons advanced by the Indian leadership for attacking the salt tax? They started by raising a doctrinal objection. They declared that it was one of the basic canons of good finance and fair taxation that a prime necessity of life, which salt obviously was, should not be taxed, and that too to such an inordinate extent.19o They then questioned the very basic formulation of the administrators that even though the tax yielded'such rich revenue its burden was not felt by the people to be at all oppressive, since it was spread over a large part of the population.191 They said that its incidence should be gauged not in the abstract but in the context of, and with reference to, the stark poverty of the people of India. When the low level of income of the people was taken into consideration, even the tax burden of a few annas per head could be seen for what it was in reality—back-breaking. Explaining this line of argument, G. V. Joshi observed that the salt tax would not have been so mischievous if, among other things, there had been .a 'steady, advancing improvement in the condition of the poorer classes, so as to leave them a larger and larger margin of incoime for their necessary expenditure'.19² Graphically illustrating this assertion, N. V. Barve, mover of the resolution on the salt tax at the Allahabad Congress of 1888, pointed out: 'There are millions on millions to whom that eight annas extra means eight meals less in a year, and that to people who, even before this enhancement, never got more than one meal in the 24 hours.'193 Some of the nationalists sought to lend added șupport to their argument by making mathematical calculations of the incidence of the salt tax. For example, in 189o, Pringle Kennedy calculated that while the average incidence of the salt tax, at an income of Rs. 5 per month for a family of 5, was 6 pies in the rupee, the income tax was only 4 pies in the rupee in the lower slab.194 Similarly, D. E. Wacha calculated in the same year that salt duty constituted 1.1 per cent of per capita income even if the latter was taken to be £2.195

The chief ground for the nationalist attack on the salt tax was its ‘unjust and vicious' character arising out of the fact that it fell most heavily, oppressively, and 'cruelly' on the poorest of the poor' of the land, who could not afford to pay any taxes, and whose income barely sufficed—or in many cases failed to suffice—to maintain body and soul together.196 This was the main reason advanced by the Indian National Congress for repeatedly opposing the enhancement of the tax.197 In this context, a few of the Indian leaders also took note of the financial inequity and the regressive character of the salt tax. This was the main ground on which, as early as 1871, Dadabhai Naoroji objected to the salt tax. In his 'Statement to the Select Committee on East India Finance', he pointed out that the incidence of its burden on the poor was not similar 'to that on the other classes for the share they pay towards revenue'. After stating that the burden of the salt tax on a poor cooly, workman, or peasant was about 4 per cent of his paltry income of 2os a year, he contended that ‘4 per cent out of 2os is far more important to the poor man than 1o or 2o per cent out of the income of the richer classes'.198 Similarly, in 188o, the anonymous writer of the article, 'The Finances of India under Lord Lytton', in the Journal of the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha, wrote that 'the fact that this tax reaches in its incidence the poor and the rich alike and not proportionately to their means, is the most powerful reason why it should be religiously kept at such a low rate as not to fall oppressively upon the poor masses'.199 Similar sentiments were expressed by other leaders.2o0 It was, however, G.V. Joshi who articulated this view most clearly and unambiguously in 1886 and argued that 'the equality in the amount paid is a false equality, it does not imply true equality—namely, the "equality of sacrifice".'zoi Two years later, in 1888, he endeavoured to show that the salt duty enhancement 'tends to aggravate the inequalities in the distribution of public taxation'as between the poor and the rich.2oz

The next test applied by the Indian leadership was that of the effect of the tax on the consumption of salt. It should be noted in this connection that the British Indian administrators were in the past equally willing to put their salt tax policy to such a test.zo3 Even James Westland had, while enhancing the tax in 1888, expressed the hope 'that the burden of a duty of Rs. 2-8 will not now have any effect in restricting the rate at which the consumption is increasing'.zo4 The lndian leaders were, on the other hand, convinced that the practical effect of the high rate at which the salt tax was maintained, and, in particular, of its enhancement in 1888, was to restrict and diminish the consumption of salt. This in turn meant tremendous suffering and hardship for the people, since salt was a commodity that was essential for the very physical existence and well-being of man.205

Once again, it was G. V. Joshi who put forward the nationalist case in a reasoned and powerful manner. As usual, his main appeal was to 'facts'. He set out to make a detailed statistical analysis of the experience of the last 19 years.2o6 He showed that changes in the rates of the tax were invariably followed by changes in consuinption, increases following reductions of duty, and decreases following enhancement of duty'.zo7 He further pointed out that it was only the very poor who felt compelled to cut down their .consumption of salt when its price increased. While the well-off or nearly well-off members of the middle and upper middle classes certainly did not have to ever stint on their salt, on the consumption of the poorest strata of the population ‘the slightest variation in the salt duty tells in a perceptible degree'. Any fall in the consumption of salt would not, therefore, be shared in by the entire population; it would be borne entirely by the poorer sections.2o8 He resumed the argument in 1896 in his essay, 'The Salt Duty Question'. Here he pointed to the experience of the years since 1888, and asserted that his gloomy prophecies had proved to be too correct. Quoting statistics, he showed that the rate of growth of the consumption of salt had not remained the same after 1888 as it was before 1888. From 1882 to 1887 this rate had been on the average 3.8 per cent per year, from 1887-8 to 1894-5 it was 0.12 per cent. Wnat was even worse, the consumption of salt had 'not even kept pace with .the growth of population during the period'. Joshi calculated that the consumption of salt per head in India, which was 8.8 lbs. in 188o-81, had increased to 10.3 lbs. in 1886-7; but that by 1894-5 it had again fallen to 9.5 lbs. He estimated that the lower 8o millions of the population were compelled in 1894-5 to ‘eat from two to three lbs. less salt per capita than they used to do 8 years ago'. And so, the conclusions he had arrived at drove him to exclaim:

A fiscal system which renders it necessary to so bleed the starving poor—no matter how great the necessities of policy —is open to grave condemnation. No canon of finance, no. principle of political economy, could ever sanction such a merciless application of the fiscal lancet; and no necessity, however imperative—and no financial emergency, however pressing,—could be pleaded in justification of such cynical indifference to the sufferings of the poor.

Then came the final judgment, born of despair and anger: 'But our financial administration knows no such weakness of common humanity and shows little sympathy with struggling poverty.'2o9

Another leader who played a prominent role in the anti-salt tax agitation after 1888 was G. K. Gokhale. He followed, or rather repeated, in all essential respects, Joshi's reasoning as well as his statistics. His contribution in this respect was that he popularised Joshi's work.210

Another charge levelled against the salt tax by the nationalists was its tendency to injure agriculture by starving land and cattle of sufficient quantities of an essential commodity such as salt.2ı1 G. V. Joshi and G. K. Gokhale, in addition, condemned the salt tax for being a tax on an industry—the salt industry-and because it resulted in a system of monopoly which affected India's ‘economic growth in an essential and vital particular'.2i2 Moreover, they alleged that various factors emanating from the imposition of the salt tax, like the government monopoly of salt, equalisation of the salt tax in different provinces, and high rates of the tax had been responsible for restricting and destroying a flourishing local industry in large parts of the country, and particularly in Bengal, and for driving the indigenous producer out of the field.213 G. V. Joshi went to the extent of making the accusation that 'the total decline of the industry in Bengal marks a transfer of it, by :skilful fiscal manipulation, to foreign hands'.2i4 He also expressed the apprehension that in Bombay too 'the fatal Bengal policy' was being pursued 'though in a disguised form, without the intermediate stage of monopoly'.215

## VIII. ALTERNATIVE SOURCES OF TAXATION

Tacitly or otherwise, many of the Indian leaders recognised that the budgetary deficits,.which had forced the government to maintain the salt tax at such a high rate, had somehow or other to be covered and that they were, therefore, obliged to suggest other ways and means, less objectionable than the salt tax, of raising the necessary additional revenue. This task, they believed, was not really a difficult one. The first alternative they recommended was the re-imposition of import duties.2i6 Another alternative measure that some of them advocated was enhancement 'of the income tax²i7 and its extension to classes that had hitherto been exempted from its. purview.zi8 A few of them pressed for a general imposition of taxes on the rich and on the products consumed by them; they did not, however, specify the forms such taxes might take.2i9 Some of them also felt that the scope for the retrenchment of the existing civil and military expenditure should have been exhausted before the decision to raise the salt tax was taken in 1888.2zo Almost all the nationalist newspapers of Bombay Presidency, for example, suggested at the time that the financial deficit might have been better met by reducing the salaries of European government servants by about one-fourth.22i

Joshi and Gokhale also pointed to the built-in fiscal mechanism within the framework of the salt tax itself that should have been relied upon to increase its yield in the long run. This mechanism was based on the well-known principle of taxation that a source of revenue like the salt tax might be made most fruitful if a low rate of tax was accompanied by unrestricted consumption. In 1896, G. V. Joshi calculated that if the consumption of salt had continued to expand after 1888 at the same rate at which it had increased before, it would have risen by nearly 1o million maunds by 1895-6 and the state would have derived at the old rate of tax of Rs. 2 per maund an increase of revenue to the extent of 2 crores of rupees—almost equal to the amount of additional revenue that had actually been obtained under the enhanced tax with its concomitant low consumption of salt.2zz This principle of taxation was commended for application to salt by Gokhale in his budget speech of 19o2;223 and, finally, clearly enunciated in his budget speech of 1903: 'The soundest and best policy in the matter—even financially—would, therefore, seem to be to raise an expanding revenue on an expanding consumption under a diminishing scale of duties.'224

The Indian leaders were angry at the government's refusal to adopt any of the alternative measures of taxation suggested by them and many of them accused the government of cowardice and partiality for bowing before the pressure of the British manufacturers, the British officials, and the influential richer strata of the Indian population, while bullying and burdening the 'helpless' and 'speechless' millions of the land. For instance, the Kesari of 24 January 1888 made the following sarcastic comment:

If His Lordship had' resorted to import duties on cotton piece-goods from Manchester, it would have created consternation among the manufacturers of Manchester....Will the kind British Government prove faithless to those who render good assistance during the elections for Parliament? If the income tax be increased its burden will mostly fall upon the high European officers and traders, and the rates of exchange being already high, they will find it very crushing and will rise in rebellion.... Will a wise man like Lord Dufferin ever venture to have his fair reputation sullied by rousing such opposition? In short, Lord Dufferin was wise in rejecting both these alternatives and in having recourse to an increase in the salt duty, remembering that the disarmed and the loyal people of India will not, as long as they are alive, say 'no' to any demand and that they need not be very strong in body as Government has graciously undertaken to protect them.225

## Similarly, the Indian Spectator of 22 January 1888 wrote :

Or perhaps they (government) are afraid of 'public opinion'. For, it is certain that in the case of such an increase in the taxation of the rich classes, Government would have a bad time of it from 'representatives of the two hundred and fifty millions' whereas these two hundred millions and odd have no voice to protest against the enhancement of the duty on salt. Whatever may be said of their sense of justice, it cannot be denied that the Government of India are wise in their generation.226

The Mahratta of 22 January, the Hindu of 25 January, the Amrita Bazar Patrika of 26 January, the Bengalee of 28 January, and the Sanjivani of 4 February 1888z7 commented in a like vein. G. V. Joshi too passed a similar judgment on the motives lying behind the government's action in the matter.z28 The Mahratta of 18 March 1888 even claimed that the real question involved in the issue of the salt tax was whether India existed for Indians or for others.

## IX. POLITICAL LESSONS

Many of the nationalist critics of the salt tax strongly censured the conduct of Peary Mohan Mookerjea and Dinshaw

Petit, the two nominated members of the Legislative Council, in extending their support to the enhancement of the tax in 1888;229 and cited it as a proof-positive of the nationalist contention that the existing Legislative Councils did not and could not reflect correctly Indian opinion or the interests of the masses of the country, and that these Councils had, therefore, to be reformed by introducing popular element in them.23o This conclusion was effectively driven home by G. V. 'Joshi who remarked that the debate on the enhancement of the tax ‘was discreditable to the Legislative Council;... that it 'was more discreditable to the native members..; and that, :above all, it was most discreditable to the system....'31 Similarly, Madan Mohan Malaviya said at the Congress of 189o: 'We would much rather that there were no non-official members at all on the Councils than that there should be members who are not in the least in touch with the people and who being ignorant of their true conditions and requirements, betray a cruel want of sympathy with them.' Describing P. M. Mookerjea and Dinshaw Petit as 'these big honourable gentlemen, enjoying private incomes and drawing huge salaries', he asked rhetorically: 'Do you think, gentlemen, such members would be appointed to the Council if the people were allowed any voice in their selection?' Amidst shouts of 'no, no, never' from the audience, he again enquired: 'And' even if they were, by some mistake, once appointed, would they not be scornfully rejected at the next election?'32

## X. CONCLUSION

The above analysis of the nationalist attitude towards the salt tax clearly shows that the demand for reduction in, and abolition of, the tax was taken up by the Indian national leadership on a national scale and as a national policy. The demand was in fact utilised to question and assail the very foundations of the prevailing financial policy. Moreover, the interests of the poor of the land were, in this instance, correctly and' volubly expressed by the nationalist leaders, who, in this way, made an attempt to carry the poor with the growing national movement. This was, in fact, explicitly understood by the Indian leaders to be so. Introducing the resolution demanding a reduction in the salt tax at the Sixth National Congress in 189o, Pringle Kennedy addressed the following exhortation to the delegates :

Fellow delegates, will you show that they accuse you falsely who say, your case is all for yourself, that thìs movement is simply one for the substitution of Indian dark-skinned for European fair-skinned Brahmins, by saying: 'If in no other way can this yoke be made less grievous, tax us, tax the rich, but let the poor go!'233

Similarly, G. S. Khaparde, a delegate to the Congress of 1892.. described the resolution on the salt tax as a poor man's. ‘prayer to the Congress'.234

Conversely, the national leadership did not, in this instance. at all represent the interests of the rich of the land; otherwise, like the rich man's spokesmen, they too would have supported the salt tax and its enhancement in the belief that it would help ward off other taxes, for example, the income tax, on the rich.235 The divergence between the nationalist stand and the interests of the rich can be seen clearly if the former is contrasted with the pro-tax attitude adopted and publicly expressed by the contemporary spokesmen of the zamindars, big merchants, and unofficial Englishmen. In 1882, Durga Charan Laha, the spokesman of Calcutta traders in the Imperial Legislative Council, opposed the ‘questionable policy' of reducing the salt tax on ‘sentimental grounds'. It would have been better, he suggested, if the government had instead removed direct taxes which were unsuited ‘to the circumnstances of the country'.236 Maharaja Jotendra Mohan Tagore, who represented the Bengal zamindars in the Council, endorsed in full Laha's stand.237 The enhancement of the salt tax in 1888 was, simnilarly, fully supported by Peary Mohan Mookerjea, another representative of the Bengal zamindars, Dinshaw Petit, the spokesman of Bombay merchants, and various spokesmen of the British officials and businessmen in India.238 Supporting the enhancement of the tax, the Report of the Committee of the Bengal National Chamber of Commerce for 1887 claimed that it was 'far less objectionable than the doubling of the present rate of the Income Tax would have been'.239 And the Secretary of the Chamber and its President for 1889 opposed the demand for the reduction of the tax with no less vigour.24o

## XI. EXCISE REVENUE

Another indirect tax and an imnportant source of revenue was the excise imposed as duty upon the manufacture and as fees for licences for the sale of intoxicating spirits, hemp drugs, and opium. The gross revenue obtained from this source increásed from Rs. 1.18 crores in 186o-01 to Rs. 3.19 crores in 188o-01 and Rs. 6.64 crores in 1902-03.241 Only that aspect of the revenue which was derived from spirits—and indigenous or country-spirits at that—and which constituted the major portion of the total excise revenue has been dealt with here.242 The system of levying excise duty on country spirits varied from place to place and time to time. These systems can, however, be roughly grouped together under two broad categories: (i) the central distillery system under which a fixed duty was inposed on each gallon of spirit mnanufactured and issued for sale; and (ii) the outstill system under which duty was levied not on the quantity produced but in the gross by lump-sum payments on the basis of auctions. Obviously, there was much less governnental check over consumption of liquor under the outstill system than under the central distillery system.243 In the 189o's it was the declared policy of the Government of India to raise a maximum of.revenue from a minimum of consumption of spirits, that is, to increase the rates of duty and to restrict the number of places for the sale of liquor to such an extent that its consumption was minimised while, at the same time, illicit manufacture of liquor was kept within bounds.244 At least after 189o, it became the settled and proclaimed policy of the government to extend the central distillery system and to gradually abolish the outstill system.245

The Indian leadership' attached a great deal of importance to the question of consumption of intoxicating liquors and their taxation. It was as a whole anti-liquor and opposed to the spread of the drinking habit in the country. It looked upon drinking as a deadly evila terrible scourge'—which was morally corrupting and 'vicious', economically impoverishing, and physically weakening.246 Some of the Indian leaders even believed that the spread of drinking would hamper the growth of industries by adversely affecting the efficiency of labour.247

But in condemning liquor-consumption, the lndian leaders once again 'struck the first blow at the government'. Putting the blame for the spread of the drinking habit squarely and primarily on the shoulders of the government and its excise policy, they censured the government for deliberately or unintentionally encouraging, or at least failing to actively discourage, drinking in order to maximise excise revenue.248 Criticism of the official excise policy waș particularly sharp and persistent in Bengal during the 188o's, because of the prevalence in the province of the outstill system, which was .accused of cheapening the price of liquor.249

The Indian leaders also alleged that even when the government publicly and professedly claimed to adhere to a policy of discouraging drinking, in practice the effect was just the reverse.25o The tendency on the part of the administration to lavish praise and promotion on officials under whose jurisdiction excise revenue increased and to pass censure on those who could produce no such results provided, in real life, an incentive to the officials to be zealous in increasing excise revenue, .even if it meant an increase in the consumption of liquor.251 It might be observed in this connection that the spokesmen of the government were no less vehement in rebutting the nationalist charge and in asserting that the increase in the excise revenue was in fact due mainly to higher rates of duty and stricter excise control.252 Interestingly enough, an increase in liquor consumption was indirectly acknowledged by government officials in certain other contexts, but then it was merely offered as an indication of the increasing prosperity of the people.253 Consistent with their entire approach to the question, the nationalists refuted this contention and maintained that, on the contrary, liquor brought ruin and destitution and misery to its imbibers and their families.254

The nationalist leaders were convinced that education would be too slow a way of fighting the evil and that only adininistrative measures could put an immediate halt to the spread of drinking.255 They therefore neglected popular agitation against drinking and concentrated their efforts on cajoling or browbeating the government to subordinate revenue considerations. to the moral well-being of the people and, hence, to follow a policy of active discouragement of drinking.256 Nearly all the administrative measures advocated by them were designed to make liquor more expensive or more difficult to obtain through a reduction in the number of distilleries and liquor shops. The measure which gained the widest favour with them was that of 'Local Option'. They demanded that in deciding the question of opening a new liquor shop or distillery at any place local public opinion expressed directly or through the local municipal bodies must be considered the decisive factor.257 Another popular demand, though confined mostly to Bengal, was for the abolition of the outstill system.258 When the outstills were abolished in the larger part of Bengal during 1889 and 189o, the Indian National Congress itself welcomed the step.259 The nationalists also favoured the enhancing of import and excise duties on liquor.26o Some of them wanted the government to take purely administrative steps like the closing of liquor shops and the adoption of 'stringent measures... in granting licences to retail liquor shops'.26ı Surprisingly enough, prohibition was not one of the remedies that found advocacy on a significant scale in the nationalist ranks. Perhaps, it was thought to be outside the realm of the possible.262

The above analysis shows that the nationalist attitude towards excise revenue derived from liquor was purely nationalistic in motivation and content. Even though liquor excise was a fruitful source of revenue, which was, moreover, paid by the actual consumers of liquor only, thus obviating the need for imposing additional taxes on the general mass of population, the nationalist leaders looked upon it solely from the point of view·of the general good as they saw it and did not hesitate to condemn it. This aspect of the nationalist policy can be viewed from another angle also. On the one hand, those among the Indian leaders who consumed liquor would be clear losers if the nationalist plea for costlier liquor was adopted. On the other hand, the nationalist demand for reducing the supply of liquor, which would result in a fall in the excise revenue, was indirectly opposed to the interests of the non-consumers of liquor as tax-payers. It is thus obvious that in this, instance we have another example of nationalistic altruism in matters of finance.

Two other aspects of the question under discussion may also be noted. Firstly, while the tendency towards utilisation of anti-liquor agitation as a powerful political weapon that the nationalist movement in India was to reveal later was not yet present, the consciousness was dawning in the ranks of the Indian leadership that anti-liquor agitation might be useful in training the people for political work. Speaking at the meeting of the Anglo-Indian Temperance Association, held on 17 April 1905, Dadabhai Naoroji observed :

They had 3oo branches of the Association in India, and this meant that people of all classes, creeds, and of every position from the top to the bottom, were learning the great lesson of associating together, becoming like brothers to each other, working for one great cause.... This association was starting not only the cause of breaking down drinking in India, but the equally good cause of raising the people to a higher position of thought and of union which would be of the greatest use to them.263 (Emphasis added)

Secondly, the very tendency to blame the foreign government for the spread of the drinking-habit lent to the nationalist excise policy a nationalist undertone.

## XII. OPIUM REVENUE

Opium was another important source of revenue for the

Government of India. Till 1884, it was the second, and after 1884 the third, most productive item in the budget. Its yield, however, fluctuated violently from year to year, thus lending a certain amount of instability to Indian finance. For example, the net opium revenue amounted to Rs. 8.45 crores in 188o-81, Rs. 5.70 in 1890-91, and Rs. 4.97 in 1900-01, while in 1897-8 it even went down to Rs. 2.70 crores.264 The opium revenue was derived from export of Bengal opium which was produced under a system of state monopoly in Bihar and N.W. Provinces and Oudh under the jurisdiction of the Bengal government and from the levy of a heavy export duty in Bombay on Malwa opium. Most of the export opium was sent to China. A part of the Bengal opium was sold in India under the excise system and revenue from its sale was credited under excise. The excise revenue from opium increased steadily from Rs: 36 lakhs in 1870-71 to Rs. 103 lakhs in 1900-01.265

The opium trade with China was criticised throughout the 19th century by various sections of the press and public opinion of Britain on moral and humanitarian grounds. In 1888, an organised movement against the opium trade and its patronage and promotion' by the government came into existence in Britain with the founding of the Society for the Suppression of the Opium Trade.266 As a result of this agitation, the House of Commons passed a resolution on 10 April 1891, affirming that the system of opium revenue in India was inmoral and urging the Government of India to 'cease to grant licences for the cultivation of the poppy and sale of opium in British India, except to supply the legitimate demand for medicinal purposes'; and in 1893 a Royal Commission was appointed to investigate the question in its entirety. The Commission reported in 1895 and advised against any disturbance of the status quo.267 All these years, the Government of India strongly objected to the proposals for prohibiting or restricting the opium trade, mainly on the ground of financial and political inexpediency of such steps.268

The attitude of the Indian leaders towards the opium trade and the revenue arising from it makes an interesting study. Here was a situation in which they had to chocse between national self-interest-which was their guiding factor in most other respects—and humanitarianism and philanthropy-on the basis of which they usually appealed to the British people and government to grant them economic and political concessions.

As regards the moral aspects of the opium revenue, most of the national leaders assumed a position that was at great variance with the official position, and nearer the position of the English critics of the revenue, since these leaders openly conceded that the opium trade and revenue were grossly immoral and deserving of severe condemnation. As early as 187o, Keshab Chandra Sen had asked for the abolition of 'that iniquitous opium traffic which kills thousands of the poor Chinese people'.269 In 188o, Dadabhai Naoroji indignantly proclaimed : 'This opium trade is a sin on England's head, and a curse on India for her share in being the instrument.'27o Writing in the same vein, the Mahratta of 7 August 1881 protested against the Indian people being made to carry on their head 'the curse of being the poisoners of 4oo millions of human beings'.271

Dadabhai Naoroji and R. C. Dutt disagreed with the officials on the nature of the opium revenue also. While the official view was that the opium revenue was paid not by the Indian people but by the Chinese consumers,272 Naoroji and Dutt averred that it was in fact a disguised tax on the people of India since, in the absence of state monopoly of opium, the governiment profits, known as opium revenue, would have accrued to the people of India.273

But on the main aspect of the opiun question, viz., abandoninent of the opium trade, differences of opinion arose within the ranks of the Indian leaders. While a large majority adopted an approach that very nearly coincided with that of the Government of India, a significant number favoured giving up of the opium trade on humanitarian grounds. Opposing the sacrifice of the opium revenue for mere sentimental reasons, the former argued that the opium revenue constituted such an important part of Indian finances that its cessation was bound to lead to the imposition of new taxes. They therefore strongly disapproved of the anti-opium agitation being carried on in England at the time.274 Thus the Amrita Bazar Patrika observed in its issue dated 9 July 188o : ‘To abandon such a source of revenue and then make good the gap by ruinous taxes wrung from the life-blood of an already impoverished people would, according to our humble ideas of morality, be in the highest degree immoral.'275 The hyper-moralistic organ of the militant Sadharana Brahmo Samaj, the Brahmo Public Opinion, warned in its issue of 15 July 188o against the fiscal hazards of taking too moralistic an attitude towards the opium revenue.276 Even the Indian Spectator, the most consistent and vehement advocate of social reforms in India, branded, on 12 March 1882, the anti-opium English agitators as ‘a powerful class of well-meaning but ignorant fanatics'.277 Another reformist newspaper, the Hindu, declared in its issue dated 3 July 1893 that it would agree to the abolition of the opium revenue only if the British tax-payers promised to make good the resulting loss to the Government of India.278

Many of the Indian leaders waxed sarcastic at what they described as the attempt of the anti-opium Englishmen to practise philanthropy at the cost of other people. They suggested that if the British people were genuine in their regard for the welfare of the Chinese people they should persuade their own government to make good the loss of the opium revenue to the Indian governmnent.279 Interestingly enough, the ranks of the anti-opium agitators in Britain, whose integrity was questioned in this fashion and who were branded as fanatics and fools, included such pro-Indian National Congress stalwarts às the Members of Parliament W.S. Caine and Samuel Smith.

This group of Indian leaders also advanced a few other interesting arguments in favour of their stand against the prohibition of the opium trade. Firstly, they contended that before starting to undertake a reform of the vices in China the authorities and reformers in England and India owed a duty to condemn and fight against the drink evil in India, which was no less pernicious than opium smoking.28o Secondly, some of them averred that any action by India resulting in the giving up of its opium exports would prove to be infructuous since the gap would be readily filled by countries like Persia, Turkey and the United States or by the Chinese themselves by extending the cultivation and manufacture of opiumn in their country. Thus, the ends of philanthropy would still remain unsatisfied, and only the Indian people would have lost a source of income.281 Curiously enough, the newspapers did not offer the argument that prohibition of the opium trade would adversely affect the Indian merchants engaged in it. On the contrary, in its issue dated 23 December 1893, the Sanjivani. an anti-opium paper, accused the merchants of supporting the opium trade because it yielded them large profits.28z The absence of trade motive from, and the predominance of the revenue motive in, the armoury of arguments advanced by the nationalist opponents of the anti-opium agitation show that the interests of opiumn merchants did not perhaps play an important role in the formation of the opium policy of this group of the national leadership.

The second group of Indian leaders, which was in favour of prohibiting opium production and banning of the opium trade out of humanitarian considerations, proposed the bridging of the consequent revenue gap by retrenchment of expenditure. In this respect the position of many newspapers like the Mahratta, the Hindu, the Som Prakash, and the Ananda Bazar Patrika was ambivalent, depending on the personal proclivities of whosoever happened to be writing the editorials at a particular moment of time. The anti-opiun nationalist sentiments were cogently and vigorously sunimed up by the Ananda Bazar Patrika of 20 July 188o as follows:

What code of ethics has taught the rulers the argument that, because China must, to satisfy her wants, necessarily import the drug from elsewhere, there is no harm in India supplying her with it? As well might a robber justifv his acts by pleading necessity, and a murderer by remarking that the person whom he had murdered would have certainly died some day, though not by his hands. The British Government can reduce the public expenditure in India by half if only it wishes to do so; it can thereby easily discontinue the inequitable trade in opium.283

Dadabhai Naoroji, who was a consistent opponent of opium trade, even though he recognised that most of the other national leaders were not one with him on this question,284 made an attempt to utilise the opium debate then going on in England to propagate the cause of Indian nationalism, thus proving once again that there was no public issue which he could not press into the service of the cause to which he had dedicated all his life and passion. Denying that opium trade benefited Inia in any manner, he claimed in 188o that the profits of this trade in reality went to meet the drain to England. India received only 'the curse of the Chinese race'. As a matter of fact, the opium revenuc merely disguised the serious economic malady and fiscal bankruptcy from which the country was suffering. If this 'cursed' opium trade had not existed, he argued, India would not have been able to meet England's claims and 'India's iniseries would have much sooner come to the surface' and been redressed. The opium trade had,

570 The Rise and Growth of Economic Nationalism in India

therefore, only prolonged the agonies of India'.285 Repeating this argument word by word in an address to a conference called by the Society for the Suppression of the Opium Trade in London in 1886, Dadabhai urged the Society to see the problem of opium in the over-all context of the main problem of India, viz., that of poverty. If India was allowed to keep what it produced and to freely develop its material resources, he averred, it could yield a large enough revenue to easily enable the government 'to dispense with the curse of the opium revenue'.286

Thus, the attitude of the national leaders towards the opium revenue was the result of the interplay of two, and in this instance conflicting, tendencies-humanitarianism and national self-interest. While some of these leaders tried to reconcile these two opposing considerations and, when forced to make a choice, embraced humnanitarianisim, many others, including M. G. Ranade. B. G. Tilak. S. N. Banerjea, and the brothers Motilal and Sisir Kumar Ghose, let the fear of fresh taxation and considerations of national self-interest triumph over their moral hesitations. At the same time, it may be noted that the Indian leadership did not offer the same type of intense and bitter and widespread and united opposition to the British agitation for the surrender of the opiuin revenue as it did from 1877 to 1882 to the agitation for the surrender of the customs revenue, even though the dimensions of the financial loss involved would have been much larger in the case of the former.287 This can perhaps only be explained by the fact that in the case of opium revenue the loss would have been only financial, not affecting any industrial interest, while in the case of the cotton import duties the loss was financial as well as industrial. Obviously the Indian leadership was of the view that, while the mere financial loss might be borne under protest, if not with equanimity and even satisfaction of having stood true to principles, the industrial loss had to be fought tooth and nail since it involved the long term and 'real interests of the nation.

# CHAPTER XII Public Finance, II

The chief difference between the official and non-official views is in regard to the channel of expenditure. — B. G. TILAK

No question presses on this country with such urgency as the question of the apalling growth of military expenditure. —D. E. WACHA

## I. EXPENDITURE

Besides examining the incidence of taxation and the mode of its assessment, the Indian national leaders also dealt at length with the manner of its application, for in their view the purposes for which taxes were imposed and the manner of their disbursement were no less important considerations than their total amount. They fully recognised that there was a big difference between tax-revenue which was expended for the benefit of the tax-payers and which might be said to have returned to the people in an indirect manner and tax-revenue which was spent unproductively and uselessly or wastefully. By applying this criterion, the Indian leaders came to the conclusion that the expenditure policy of the Government of India was unsatisfactory and even injurious to the interests of the people. Firstly, they took exception to the fact that a large part of Indian revenues was spent not inside but outside the country and was thus 'drained' out of it, and pointed out that in this respect India's case was very different from that of an independent country, like Britain, where also heavy taxation might be imposed.1 This aspect has been discussed at length in the chapter on 'The Drain'. Here it may only be pointed out that some Indian leaders laid more stress on the physical direction of the expenditure of public revenues than on the totality of their impost. Thus, in 188o, Dadabhai Naoroji maintained that 'the real question, the most important question of all questions, at present is, not how to get £6o,ooo,ooo or £ioo,ooo,ooo, for the matter of that, if that be necessary, but how to return to the pcoplc what is raised from them'.2 In a letter, written in September 1887, he was even more explicit: 'It is not the incidence of taxation that is India's evil. It would still be of no consequence if as much Land Revenue as Akbar's were taken. The evil is the drain of a portion of the revenue out of the country'.3 The Amrita Bazar Patrika also made a similar contention in its issue dated 22 February 19oo:

If the Indians are taxed highly and the money raised thereby is spent in the country, the people do not get exhausted. But if they are made to send out annually 25 crores worth of the produce of the soil to England for which they get back nothing in return, they become impoverished even if lightly taxed.4

Secondly, the nationalist leaders found the public expenditure to be extravagant and its nature and distribution unsuited to and unconnected with the true needs and condition of the people and the country's economy. They believed that it could be 'better and more profitably' applied. While most often this conclusion was only implied in their criticism of the expenditure of Indian revenues, sometimes, especially in the case of the economists among them, it was clearly grasped and articulated and made the basis of their analysis. Thus, in its issue dated 30 March 1882, the Amrita Bazar Patrika expressed the opinion that in any examination of the fnancial system of the country the real question to be asked was 'How much of the amount raised is spent on the country, and how much simply thrown away?' And the true remedy, in its view, was that 'taxes must be applied for the good of those from whom they are raised'. While conceding that the Indians had to pay 'for the maintenance of British rule in India', the Patrika protested against the use of Indian revenues to satisfy 'the claims of all parties—Cheshire,. Manchester, London, Civil Service, the Military Service, the office seeker and the adventurer' or 'to aid England in her excursions of aggression and spoliation'. While acknowledging the utility of the construction of 'roads and bridges and dispensaries and schools and chowkies' all over the country, Pherozeshah Mehta asked in 1883 the more fundamental questions whether the expenditure·of resources on these improvements had not been excessive and misdirected, 'whether the same resources could not have been better and more profitably employed, whether gross blunders might not have been avoided by a better knowledge of real wants?'5 During his first budget speech in the Bombay Legislative Council in 1895, B. G. Tilak laid down the principle that the proper criterion for evaluating the budget should be 'how far the revenue has increased during the last 25 years and what portion of it has been devoted to the material development of the Province'.6 In 1896, G. V. Joshi, after statistically analysing in his essay on 'The Present Financial Position' the growth of Indian expenditure since 1883-4, opined that if 'this enormous extra expenditure' had been ‘properly applied to its legitimate purposes in promotion of internal progress and development', it might have resulted ‘in a large increase in the happiness and contentment of the teeming millions of the land'.7 Dadabhai Naoroji made a very clear statement of this position in his letter, dated 21 March 1896, to the Welby Commission.8

It was, however, left to G. K. Gokhale to put the nationalist point of view in a clear-cut theoretical form and then to base on it almost his entire analysis of Indian finance before the Welby Commission and in his famous budget speeches. Arguing before the Welby Commission in 1897 that 'an increase of expenditure, taken by itself as a feature of national finance is not necessarily open to any serious objection', he laid down the principle of public finance that 'Everything depends in this matter on the nature of the purposes for which the increase has been incurred and the results produced by such outlay of public money'. He acknowledged that national expenditure in nearly all the European countries had been increasing for the last many decades. But increases in their expenditure, he contended, differed ‘in a most fundamental respect' from the increase in Indian expenditure. Whereas increased expenditure in other countries had brought ‘increased strength and security to the nations, and increased enlightenment and prosperity to the people', India's continually growing expenditure had 'under autocratic management, defective constitutional control, and the inherent defects of alien domination, only helped to bring about a constantly increasing exploitation of our resources, has retarded our material progress, weakened our natural defences, and burdened us with undefined and indefinite financial liabilities'.9 Another basic difference between the public expenditure of India and that of other countries was that while the latter was administered solely in the interests of the tax-payers, in the case of the former 'other interests are often deemed to be quite of equal importance, and sometimęs, indeed, they are allowed to take precedence of the interests of the Indian people'. Thus, for example, the Indian revenues had to meet 'the standing claims of the interests of British Supremacy'; they had to look after 'the interests of the extension of British dominion in the East' and the interests of the European Civil and Military Services in India. Moreover, 'the interests of British commerce and of British commercial and moneyed classes often prevail over the interests of the Indian taxpayers'.10 f

The Indian leadership also contrasted, as in the case of taxation and often as a parallel argument, India's public expenditure with its poverty and its low taxable capacity, and arrived at the conclusion that the former was beyond the capacity and resources of a poor country like India. Thus. Madan Mohan Malaviya declared in 1889 that in view of the poverty of India, the growth in its expenditure was 'not only unjustifiable, but positively sinful'. He agreed that an increase in public expenditure was not bad in itself. It would be most welcome ‘if it followed upon an increase of wealth and prosperity among the people' as, for example, was the case in England. But the tragedy of India was that its public expenditure ‘goes on increasing while the condition of the people is deteriorating day by day'.11 Following the same line of reasoning, the Indian National Congress of 1894 asserted that no enquiry into the financial expenditure could bear fruit unless it included an enquiry ‘into the ability of the Indian people to bear their existing financial burdens'.1a Pherozeshah Mehta also explained this point of view at length in his speech in the Imperial Legislative Council of 1895-6. He pointed out that the necessity of expenditure was after all a relative term, for 'however great the necessity for a'particular item of expenditure, that necessity must be controlled by the ways and means for incurring it'. Emphasising that the tax burden was ultimately carried by the poor ryot, he pronounced his firm belief that ‘if revenue can only be raised in this manner, the expenditure for which this revenue is required to be raised, however academically reasonable or incontrovertible in itself, is beyond the capacity and resources of the country'.12

Believing, therefore, that the growth of public expenditure during the past many years was disproportionate, extremely harmful, and even one of the causes responsible for India's poverty, the Indian leadership supported, and agitated for, the retrenchment of public expenditure on an extensive scale and declared such retrenchment to be an essential remedy for India's financial and economic ills. The Indian National Congress at its very first session suggested that the projected increase in military expenditure ought to be met by retrenchment of other public expenditure.13 Retrenchment was once again proffered as a remedy for financial difficulties by the Congress at its third session in 1887.14 In 1891, the Congress declared that the extravagant cost of military and civil administration was one of the important causes of the poverty of India and that one of the remedies of this poverty was the curtailment of public expenditure.15 This correlation between public expenditure, poverty and famine, and retrenchment was repeatedly stressed by the Congress in 1892, 1896, 1897, 1899, 1901, and 1904.16 In 1894, 1895, and 1897, it again recorded ‘its firm conviction'that reduction of expenditure was the only remedy for the embarrassed condition of the finances of the country.17 Similar and equally powerful pleas for a curtailment of expenditure were made by the National Conference that met in Calcutta in 188518, the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha, the Bombay Presidency Association,19 and the Madras Mahajan Sabhaiga in long memoranda submitted in 1886, G. V. Joshi in the article 'A Note on Retrenchment' published in 1886-87,20 G. K. Gokhale and D. E. Wacha in their evidence before the Welby Commission,21 and by R. C. Dutt in 1898.22

It may be of some interest to note here that, surprisingly enough, Dadabhai Naoroji did not pay much attention to retrenchment. This was, it may be suggested, because of the fact that he placed sole emphasis on production of wealth as a means of removing India's poverty. He believed that India could meet any amount of necessary expenditure provided national production was increased.23 He therefore laid emphasis on the need to stop the drain of wealth and not on the need to reduce high expenditure. Even the large-scale employment of Europeans was criticised by him more on the ground of the consequent drain than on the ground of the costliness of administration.

Thus, being vehement critics of the existing high level of public expenditure and full-throated advocates of retrenchment in it, the national leaders set out to examine the actual course of the growth of public expenditure and to suggest concrete means of limiting it. In this respect they were convinced that the high rate of public expenditure was due to preventible causes and that it could therefore be remedied.24

Another fact to be·noted about their approach to expenditure is that they showed little concern with its details, their interest being in the main confined to the basic policies governing it. Thus, for example, the Indian National Congress represented in 1895 that the enquiry by the Expenditure Čommission would prove useless 'unless the lines of policy which regulate expenditure are enquired into'.25 Similarly, Dadabhai Naoroji told the Welby Commission that he was not interested in examining the details of departmental expenditure because, in the absence of the whole administration of expenditure being put on a natural basis', such examination would be nothing more than so much 'palliating with symptoms'. What was needed, he said, was a discussion of the principles upon which the entire expenditure was conducted.26 He also felt that non-officials in India could not criticise details or go minutely into precise figures of expenditure because of paucity of information. In fact, he remarked, they did not even know which questions to ask regarding the details of expenditure. They could therefore only criticise the broad lines of policy.27

## II. MILITARY EXPENDITURE

By far the largest head of expenditure in the Indian budget during the years 188o-1905 was that of military expenditure. In 1881-2, the total net ordinary expenditure on the army and military works amounted to Rs. 17.88 crores or nearly 41.9% of the total net expenditure of the Government of India. From 1885 onwards it started going up mainly due to the Burma War, the fear of Russian advance in the north-west, the various measures for improvement and modernisation of the army undertaken from time to time, and the loss of exchange on payments made in England under this head. The steep increase in military expenditure during the period under study may be gauged from the fact that in 1886-7 it amounted to. Rs. 19.41 crores or nearly 42.4 per cent of the total net expenditure, in 1891-2 to Rs. 22.57 crores or nearly 45.4 per cent, in 1901-02 to Rs. 23.55 crores or nearly 45.2 per cent, and in 1904-05 to Rs. 30.22 crores or nearly 51.9 per cent of the total net expenditure.28 In addition, India paid a subsidy varying from £70,000 in 1869 to £100,000 in 1900 to the Royal Navy for undertaking the general naval defence of the country and also maintained local Royal Indian Marine at the same time.28a Moreover, extraordinary expenditure had to be incurred in case of wars, special campaigns and special defence works. Between the years 1876-7 and 1902-03 Rs. 22.2 crores were spent on military operations and Rs. 4.5 on special defences.29

The·Indian leaders launched a powerful attack upon mili tary expenditure and carried on a regular, intense and unanimous campaign around this issue; in fact, military expenditure was one of the most popular targets of nationalist agitation throughout the period under study. Though military expenditure in all its aspects—its amount, as well as the purpose and the manner in which it was utilised—was condemned by them, yet they concentrated their attention mainly on the financial aspect of the question; for instance, the problems of military organisation and efficiency and of military policy were more or less ignored by them except in so far as they had financial implications. The one exception to this tendency was the desire to Indianise the army.

In brief, the Indian leadership declared the heavy and ever-'growing military expenditure to be the chief cause of India's financial disequilibrium and the raison d'être of high taxation, and maintained that no improvement in finances was possible as long as unnecessary military expenditure went on climbing up or remained as high as it was. From its very first session the Indian National Congress protested against the increase of military expenditure.3o In 1889, it laid stress on 'the necessity for the reduction of, instead of the continual increase to, the military expenditure of the country'.31 In 1891, it put forward the view that the extreme poverty of the country was due to the extravagant cost of the present administration, military :and civil, but especially the former'.32 In the ensuing years, it repeatedly demanded containment of and reduction in military expenditure. Other public bodies of nationalists also took up the question from time to time. In 1885, the British India Association and the Indian Association of Calcutta, the Bombay Presidency Association, the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha, the Mahajan Sabha of Madras, the Sindh Sabha of Karachi and the Praja-hita-Vardhak Sabha of Surat jointly published and distributed a series of leaflets in England under the geueral heading 'India's Appeal to the English Electors'.33 Leaflet Number 9, entitled 21% Growth of Indian Military Expenditure in 2o Years, represented that military expenditure, which had gone up from £11,463,000 in 1857 to £16,975,750 in 1884 coùld be safely reduced to £14 millions.34

Many of the prominent nationalist publicmen dealt with military expenditure at length and with pungency. D. E. Wacha was undoubtedly the chief nationalist spokesman on the question. His writings and speeches on the subject would, if collected at one place, form a tidy volume. As early as 18S5, he reviewed, while addressing the first Indian National Congress, the growth of military expenditure since 1871 and denounced it for ‘its apalling enormnity'.35 Making another long examination at the Congress session of 1891, he asserted that 'no question presses on this country with such urgency as this question of the apalling growth of military expenditure' which was 'eating away the vitals of the people'.36 He put forward this point of view again and again in the eusuing years.37

G. V. Joshi first examined the 'disastrous influence' of the expanding military expenditure on the finances of the country in his essays on 'A Note on Retrenchment' and 'The Native Indian Army', published in 1886.38 He reiterated the charge, on the basis of detailed statistics, in his paper on 'The Present Financial Position', published in 1896.39 G. K. Gokhale was another national leader who, throughout his life, 'kept up a campaign against excessive military expenditure'.4o Many other nationalist publicmen spoke and wrote against military expenditure with equal fire and fury.41 Similarly, the nationalist newspapers kept up a continuous uproar against military expenditure and pressed for its reduction.4²

The Indian leadership based its criticism of military expenditure not only on the ground of its size but also on the ground of its disproportionateness considering the paucity of Indian resources. They laid stress on the postulate that the defence and military needs of a country could not be the only criterion for determining the proper size of its military expenditure, and that its capacity to bear the consequent burden had also to be given due weight.43 They therefore argued that India could not afford to spend as much on its armed forces as it was doing.44 Many of them represented that there was no stronger stricture on military expenditure than the fact that poor though India was it was spending a larger portion of its annual revenue on its army than did most other more advanced, richer, and militaristic nations of the world, including, Britain and Czarist Russia.45 They also cited the fact of the military expenditure absorbing the whole of the net land revenue of India as another proof of India's incapacity to afford the costly defence machine that was its armed forces.46 'The ryot is bled', remarked D.E.
