Whatever the economic significance of the drain theory, its real importance for the Indian national movement lay in its political implications, for it laid bare and enabled the Indians to arrive at the chief contradiction of the Indian situation of the time, namely, the contradiction between the Indian people and British imperialism. The process of this realisation may best be studied by tracing the manner in which the formulation and propagation of the drain theory moulded in the end the political outlook of its chief theoretician, Dadabhai Naoroji.

First of all, the very definition of the drain and the theory of its causation led Dadabhai to deny that it was the consequence and accompaniment of India's economic backwardness and to assert instead that it was all due to India's political position or to the fact that India was being ruled by a foreign power. In his essay on 'The Poverty of India' he laid stress on the fact that Britain was able to keep back a large part of India's exports chiefly because of 'the political position it holds over India'.186 In his letters to Lord Welby, written in 1896, he repeatedly emphasised that the drain was 'no simple matter of business to us' but was all simply the result of 'the unnatural administration and management of expenditure' of India's resources by an alien country.187 So far as charges in England were concerned, they were, he contended, 'forced upon India by sheer tyranny and without any voice or consent of India'.188 The entire political aspect of the question led him to exclaim: 'Truly has Lord Macaulay said: "The heaviest of all yokes is the yoke of the stranger."'189 Later, in 1897, in the course of his cross-examination before the Welby Commission, he referred to the political roots of the drain in unambiguous terms when he remarked that 'the inherent and essential defect' of British rule in India was ‘the financial, political, and intellectual drain, which is inseparable from a remote foreign dominion exercised in disregard of the sound principles' that the Indians must have their full share of public employnient and a voice in their own expenditure.190

The conclusions emerging from a pursuit of the drain theory slowly began to colour Dadabhai's understanding of and his attitude towards the nature and purposes of British rule in India. While generally proclaiming the almost providential character of British rule he began to speak a different language when dealing with the drain. Slowly and almost against his own wishes·he began to feel and also announce publicly that the beneficial, benevolent, and philanthropic nature of British rule was in reality a myth. As has already been brought out in the earlier part of this chapter, according to Dadabhai the responsibility for the lack of industrial growth and the poverty of the people lay entirely on the drain and the policy of the rulers resulting in such drain. He denied that the other supposed benefits of British Rule, viz., law and order, security of life and property, protection against foreign aggression, and protection against famine, were of any avail to Indian people when accompanied by the drain. In so far as anti-famine measures were concerned, he opined in i88o that the rulers of India could not claim any real credit for them since they themselves were ainly responsible for the famines for it was 'the drain of India's wealth by them that lays at their door the dreadful results of misery, starvation, and deaths of millions'.191 Nor did India derive any real benefit from the security that British rule provided against external aggression for 'the English rulers stand sentinel at the front door of India, challenging the whole world, that they do and shall protect India against all comers, and themselves carry away by a back-door the very treasure they stand sentinel to protect'.192 As a matter of fact, far from protecting the country,

British rule was ‘an everlasting, increasing, and every day increasing foreign invasion' that was ‘utterly, though gradually, destroying the country'.193 England was, in fact, 'the worst foreign invader she has had the misfortune to have':194 As regards the benefits of security of life and property, Dadabhai had the following remarks to make:

The romance is that there is security of life and property in India; the reality is that there is no such thing. There is security of life and property in one sense or way—i.e., the people are secure from any violence from each other or from Native despots. So far there is real security of life and property, and for which India never denies her gratitude. But from England's own grasp there is no security of property at all, and, as a consequence, no security for life. India's property is not secure. What is secure, and well secure, is that England is perfectly safe and secure, and does so with perfect security, to carry away from India, and to eat up in India, her property at the present rate.of some £ 30,o0o,o00 or £ 40,oo0,ooo a year.... I therefore venture to submit that India does not enjoy security of her property and life, and also, moreover, of 'knowledge' or 'wisdom'. To millions in India life is simply 'half-feeding', or starvation, or famines and disease.195

## With regard to law and order, Dadabhai observed in 1896:

There is an Indian saying: 'Pray strike on the back, but don't strike on the belly.'Under the native despot the people keep and enjoy what they produce, though at times they suffer some violence on the back. Under the British Indian despot the man is at peace, there is no violence; his substance is drained away, unseen, peaceably and subtly — he starves in peace and perishes in peace, with law and order!196

And added: 'I wonder how the English people would like such a fate.'

The drain theory had a deep impact on Dadabhai's understanding of the nature and character of British rule in India. Thus, he remarked near the conclusion of his paper on 'The Poverty of India' that owing to the drain ‘the whole rule moves in a wrong, unnatural, and suicidal groove'.197 In his famous speech in the House of Commons on 12 February 1895, he declared that ‘British India was indeed British India, and not India's India',198 and added: 'In a way a great mass of the Indians were worse off than the slaves of the Southern States. . . . The slaves worked on their masters' land and resources, and the masters took the profits. Indians have to work on their own land and resources and hand the profits to the foreign masters.'i99 Similarly, in a letter to Lord Welby, dated 31 January 1897, Dadabhai pleaded: 'They (British people) call us fellow-citizens, and they must make their word a reality, instead of what it is at present, an untruth and a romance—simply a relationship of slaveholder and slave.'zoo Dadabhai was, therefore, led to exclaim that the 'face of beneficence' was a mask behind which exploitation of the country was carried on by the British though 'unaccompanied with any open compulsion or violence to person or property which the world can see and be horrified with...'zoi and to assert that ‘under the present evil and unrighteous administration of Indian expenditure, the romance is the beneficence of the British Rule, the reality is the "bleeding" of the British rule'.2o2 By 1904, Dadabhai had nothing but hard and harsh words left to describe British rule over India. In his speech at the International Socialist Congress held at the Hague in August 1904, he described the British rule as barbaric, the words actually used by him being:

What did barbarity mean? Did it not mean that, when a savage knocked down a weaker man and robbed him, an act of barbarism had been perpetrated? The same applied to nations, and this was the way in which the British Government was treating India. This must end. Imperialism of brute force was barbarism.203

The drain theory deepened Dadabhai's political understanding in another way too. He slowly began to give up the traditional nationalist belief that everything that went wrong in India was the fault of the British officials in India who were selfish and who perverted the wishes of the Home authorities. In his crossexamination before the Welby Commission he laid emphasis not on the evil working of the officials but on the evil machinery or system of government which produced the drain. Thus he said:

The general attack I made is not so much upon the Government as officials, but upon the system upon which they are working, that system being an evil system. They cannot help but do what of course would produce unsatisfactory results.... It is the cause of the evil system upon which they are working, and which requires to be considered and modified so that they themselves may be able to do their work with greater satisfaction.... 204

It may also be pointed out that while Dadabhai derived his understanding of the nature of British rule from his theory of the drain, some other nationalist leaders utilised the theory to expose the exploitative character of British rule. The Amrita Bazar Patrika of 9 June 19oo, for example, defended the drain theory on the ground that it provided a visible refutation of the theory of white man's burden:

It must be always borne in mind that the understanding is that England should never exact tribute from India. The white man, it is believed in England, bears the burden of the coloured people, but never makes the latter bear his own burden. The British Government will never admit that it takes a pie from India. But if it is now shown that England not only takes a tribute but a very large one, the English people will be obliged to feel that they have some reparation to make to their dependent and helpless ward.

The understanding regarding the nature, character, and purposes of British rule in India that Dadabhai had arrived at in the course of his life-long campaign against the drain was bound to lead him on to the road of political militancy. And so the man who started his political career with the most moderate of political demands became slowly, year by year and degree by degree, more and more extreme in his politics and political demands.

On the narrow plane of administrative services, Dadabhai habitually referred to the European employees of the Government of India as ‘leeches',2o5 and as early as 1885 he put forward the demand that only 'the highest power of control and supervision'should remain in English hands and that all the other services should be manned by Indians.2o6 In 1897, he spelled out this demand further and asked that only the Viceroy, the Governors, and the Lieutenant-Governors should be English.207

Dadabhai was slow in putting forward radical demands or slogans on a higher, political level, and initially expressed his growing extremism in the negative form of issuing warnings of dire consequences that would follow if the drain was not checked. Gradually and imperceptibly, however, these warnings were transformed into direct political slogans and demands. As early as 188o, he cautioned the British against comparing themselves with the past Indian rulers. 'If the British do not show themselves to be vastly superior in proportion to their superior enlightenment and civilisation, if India does not prosper and progress under them far more largely,' he declared, 'there will be no justification for their existence in India.'Moreover, the past drain might be forgiven and forgotten as a misfortune but a similar drain in the future after the issue had been clarified would, 'in plain English, be deliberate plunder and destruction'.2o8 In 1896, he observed that, though Indian people still had an abiding faith in the soundness of the conscience of British people, 'the result of the present system would be to make the Indians to come to the conclusion that the Indians shall never have any chance “except by their getting rid in the first instance of their European rulers"'.zo9 And, again, if the drain continued British rule would also continue to be ‘a foreign crushing tyranny, leading the people to yearn.., to get rid of their European rulers',2io In his letter to Lord Welby on 'The Causes of Discontent', the great protagonist of loyalty to British rule, who had loudly declaimed at the second session of the Indian National Congress: 'Let us speak out like men and proclaim that we are loyal to the backbone',21 thundered forth even more eloquently: 'Is it possible for any sane man to think that any one nation can hold another in slavery and yet expect loyal devotion and attachment from it? It is not nature, not human nature. It has never happened and will never happen'.21z By 19oo, sentiments of disloyalty were clearly creeping into the loyal mind of Dadabhai, though perhaps unconsciously. In that year, he gave another warning to the rulers that Indians had stood their exploitation by the British too long and that it would be wrong to believe that their 'loyalty cannot fail, that it will continue in the same condition in which it is at the present'.2i3 The Indian people 'were now beginning to understand the position and might be tempted, unless something was done to ameliorate their condition, to use force in order to destroy force'.2i4 It was now just a matter of time before Dadabhai would arrive at the inevitable conclusion: India must be politically free if the drain was to be checked; and it was a great tribute to the freshness, youth, and vigour of Dadabhai's heart and brain that at the ripe age of 79, when the arteries are hardened to the last drop, he made the qualitative leap from a belief in the lasting nature of British rule over Indiazis to the demand for selfgovernment. In the famous speech on the drain at the International Socialist Congress in 19o4, which created a veritable tremor in the nationalist circles, particularly moderate circles, in India, Dadabhai declared his political faith in the following unambiguous terms:

The remedy was to give India self-government. She should be treated like other British colonies. The Indians would maintain their connexion with England, but they resented being treated as slaves. They demanded the right to govern themselves, and to share in the general progress made by the nations of the earth.216

That this declaration of his was no sudden outburst inspired by the radical atmosphere of the Socialist Congress was proved when a year later, in his message to the Benares session of the Indian National Congress, Dadabhai categorically asserted :

Without self-government the Indians can never get rid of their present drain, and the consequent impoverishment, misery, and destruction. No palliative of any kind whatever, no mere alteration and tinkering of the mechanical machinery of administration, can and will do any good at all. The drain can only be stopped by the Government, by the people themselves.... Self-government is the only remedy for India's woes and wrongs:217

It should also be remembered that it was Dadabhai, who proclaimed in his Presidential Address to the historic Calcutta session of the Indian National Congress, the future goal' of the national movement when he declared that all the political demands of the Indian people could be summed up in one word "self-government" or Swaraj, like that of the United Kingdom or the colonies'.218

The political significance of Dadabhai's extreme view that the drain was the most important cause of India's poverty may be brought out even more clearly if it is compared with the political significance of the other points of view which looked upon lack of modern industry, or excess of land revenue, or over-taxation, or absence of political rights, as the chief cause of this poverty. Before making such a comparison, however, two aspects of the comparison need to be kept in view for the price of ignoring them would be loss of proper perspective. Firstly, in the ultimate analysis even Dadabhai attributed the poverty of India to lack of industry — the only difference being that he traced the latter to the drain. Secondly, even what may be called the 'industry theory' of poverty, when pushed to its logical conclusion, led to the emergence of the radical nationalist-political inference that the British Indian Government worked for the interests of British trade and industry and that Indian industry could not, therefore, fully come into its own till a political regime was established which would primarily serve Indian industrial interests. Consequently, many of the nationalist radicals of the late 19th and early zoth centuries, such as B. G. Tilak, Lajpat Rai, and B. C. Pal, campaigned vigorously for the removal of all restrictions on the growth of Indian industry. Similarly, the 'land revenue theory' and the 'taxation theory' of poverty also sometimes led to radical political demands. All this has, however, already been brought out in this study.

The central difference between the political consequences of the drain theory and those of all the other theories of India's poverty lay in the fact that while the latter could to some extent and for some time be reconciled with the existence of foreign rule the drain theory could not be.

Take the 'industry theory', for example. While highlighting and bringing out in many ways the conflict that arose because of the clash between the industrial interests of India and those of Britain, it, at the same time, tended in several other ways to soften the political impact of this conflict. Firstly, according to the reckoning of the supporters of the ‘industry theory', British regime was not an unsurmountable barrier in the path of India's industrialisation. Some industrial growth could occur even under the existing political and economic conditions. There was, therefore, no inherent and total compulsion to link the struggle for industry with the struggle for selfgovernment. Political freedom, for example, was not one of the minimal conditions that Justice Ranade laid down for the growth of Indian industry, in spite of his emphasis on state aid.219

Secondly, the whole issue was made more complicated by the fact that the British Indian Government itself often spoke in favour of industrialisation and sometimes showed greater verbal zeal for it than was exhibited even by the Indians themselves.zzo Moreover, the Government of India did take some steps, however paltry, for the development of industry, culminating in the creation of a separate imperial Department of Commerce and Industry in 19o5.zzı In any case, there always existed the possibility that government policy towards tariffs, stores, technical education, state-aid, etc., could be changed in the direction sought by the Indians, as happened, to a limited extent, after 1918 following the recommendations of the Indian Industrial Commission.2zz Once a change in the direction of government policy had taken place, the government could no longer be declared to be prima facie anti-industry. From the very beginning, the proponents of the industry theory had had to argue in a complex way and try to show that what the government was doing was not enough and that a purely Indian Government would have done more. This was not an easy task and had to be undertaken with the help of experts, and in politics, when a case has to be argued with an expert's aid, it is as good as lost so far as the mass of people are concerned. Consequently, while the emphasis on industrial growth as the chief remedy for India's poverty could make a political appeal to the educated classes, it could not become an instrument for deepening the political cleavage between the people and the government. On the contrary, the spokesmen of the government could plausibly argue that industry provided a field of common endeavour for both the government and the nationalists.223 Thirdly, the propounders of the ‘industry theory' had to concede that while a part of the blame for industrial backwardness of the country could\`justifiably be laid on the rulers, another large part of it had to be borne by the Indians themselves who did not properly harness the existing resources of capital, men, enterprise, and technique.2z4 The conclusion was obvious: The Indians therefore must not fritter away all their energy in criticising and blaming the government; they must take a constructive approach; and expend a part of their energy in self-effort.2z5 The diluting effect of the self-effort approach on the nationalists' political and economic agitation against the government was fully recognised by many of the government spokesmen, who tried to utilise it to shift the blame for the industrial and economic backwardness of the country from their own shoulders to those of the Indians themselves. Thus, for example, after declaring in i888 that 'by removing restrictions on trade and by the multiplication of roads, railways, and the facilities of conveyance, we can foster manufacturing and mercantile activity, which we are doing; but the actual creation of manufacturing centres must be the work of private enterprise', Dufferin could chide the leaders of the Indian National Congress: 'But what the Government cannot do, the gentlemen to whom I am referring might very usefully employ themselves in doing.'zz6 It is of some interest in this respect to record an interesting incident from the history of the Indian National Congress. The Congress appointed in 19o1 a committee to report to its next session on the desirability of adopting two resolutions which would place a major share of responsibility for the ‘state of economic depression of the country' on 'want of knowledge of methods of production and distribution', urge the Indians to acquire and spread such knowledge, acknowledge that organisation of capital and credit was ‘one of the most important economic questions that required solution at our hands' and urge the Indians 'to make sustained and extensive efforts to organise capital and remove one of the many difficulties in the way of improvement of our economic condition'.227 In these resolutions was to be embodied the very spirit of Justice Ranade's famous address at the first Industrial Conference held at Poona in 189o; their promoters perhaps wanted to pay homage to the memory of the man who had inspired the Congress and the country for nearly three decades. But the resolutions were never passed. We do not know the reasons for their rejection by the committee or the Congress. It can, however, be surmised that the leaders of the Congress must have recognised that the resolutions, if adopted, would tend to divert nationalist energy away from the main task of building up a steady stream of nationalist pressure against the government. The Congress therefore stuck to its negative, non-constructive, agitatiónal approach and continued to cast the blame for India's poverty on factors namely, decline of indigenous manufactures, the drain of wealth, excessive taxation and excessively costly character of administration, and over-assessment of land—28 for which the government alone could be held responsible.

Thus, by leading to the acceptance of British rule and some of its basic economic policies and to the belief that the chief weakness of the Indian economy, viz., industrial backwardness, was primarily the product of India's own mistakes and was therefore capable of being removed by self-effort, the ‘industry theory' gave birth to the 'optimistic school of Indian economics' and served, at least initially and for some time, as the economicideological base of moderate politics.229

Similarly, even an extreme critic of the land revenue policy of the government could be satisfied by reduction in land revenue or by checking its future growth or could even be outmanoeuvered in public estimation by bringing out the element of exaggeration in his stand, as was done in 19o1 in the case of R. C. Dutt by Curzon through his Resolution on Land Revenue. The government could also to some extent appease the nationalist critic of taxation and expenditure by easing the tax burden here and there to a limited extent and by increasing by a small amount public expenditure on some welfare and developmental activity or department. Criticism of railways, however justified it might have been earlier, was slowly corroded by their increasing usefulness and profitability. Even extremism arising out of demands for national political advancement could be, with some effort, moderated by giving carefully calculated doses of political concessions and reforms. And in any case, while the nationalist could still argue his case and try to prove that the British economic policy in all these respects as well as in respect of foreign trade, currency, labour, etc., was selfish and against the interests of Indian people, it would require a certain degree of political and economic insight to penetrate through the haze of official arguments and apologia. This insight might be possessed 'by the leadership; but to impart it to the mass of people would be a long and arduous task, if a possible task at all.

This role of the various theories other than the drain theory in diluting political militancy was taken note of by Dadabhai Naoroji and his fellow campaigner of the drain theory, the Amrita Bazar Patrika. Thus, in 19oo, Dadabhai urged the youngmen of India to be on their guard against being taken in by the reasons and excuses, such as decadence of indigenous industries and excessive taxation of land, advanced for the poverty of India. 'But these, after all,· are only red herrings drawn across the path,’ he warned; and added: ‘As long as the bleeding goes on, there can be no hope for India.'z3o According to Dadabhai's biographer, R. P. Masani, Dadabhai warned R. C. Dutt in 19o3 that his emphasis on the defects in the land revenue system was likely to 'divert attention from the fundamental cause of Indian poverty — the drain — and its remedy, self-government'.231 Similarly, the Amrita Bazar Patrika observed in its issue of 9 June 19oo that, while it was undisputable that excessive land revenue was a cause of India's poverty, it was still wrong to lay more emphasis on it than on the drain. 'To raise the question of high settlement is to raise a point which is likely to be disputed, but there can be no dispute about this annual drain'.232 On 28 March 1901, the Patrika expressed the misgivings that by laying stress on the land revenue Dutt was 'raising a side-issue and would find himself lost in a labyrinth of discussion', and advised : 'What is necessary to do is to raise clear issues and stand upon firm footing to be able to bring the matter home'.233 11. lin thoor

As opposed to the 'industry theory', etc., the drain theory traced the origins of the economic evils from which India was suffering to British rule itself, it uncompromisingly highlighted the foreign nature of the British Indian Government and its exploitative character. The drain accordingly was, by its very definition, entirely the fault of Britain; and the British must bear the entire blame for the poverty arising out of it. In the situation, according to this view, the Indian people and their leaders could obviously do little that was constructive to reduce the drain; all that was within their realm was to agitate for Indianisation, fairer apportionment of India's expenditure between Britain and India, reduction of the Home Charges, etc. Furthermore, the proponents of the drain theory not only maintained that India could make no economic progress as long as the drain continued and argued that the drain was all Britain's fault, but, by so defining the drain as to make it synonymous with British rule and its basic economic and political policies, institutions, and infra-structure—namely, the British Indian Civil Service, the British army, the India Office in England, British capital investments in India, railways, foreign trade, the land revenue and taxation in general—they slowly led the people to believe that if India was an economically backward and poor country it was mainly because of 'the presence and the policy of the foreigner' and that the chief barrier in the path of the economic progress of the country was not its internal economic weaknesses but British rule itself. Even the over-emphasis on the European character of the Civil Service and the army served a similar political purpose, since, on the one hand, it helped keep before the people a visible badge of the political and economic subjugation of the country, and on the other, it led to a demand, viz. that India should have British rule but without the presence of the British themselves, which could not possibly have been accepted by the rulers and which could only succeed in arousing political passions against the rulers.

Thus the drain theory set out an acute problem, proposed solutions which would cut at the very roots of British rule in India, and posed a contradiction that could not but result in the emergence of an extremist political outlook, a total political clash between the government and the people, and, finally, the revolutionary political demand for the overthrow of British rule. Even political reforms would not satisfy the supporters of the drain theory since these reforms would not by themselves lessen the drain. Political militancy based on the drain theory would not therefore be lessened even by political reforms.234 Moreover, the drain theory glossed over all internal conflicts within Indian society and concentrated all its energy on the one evil--the drain and the foreign rule that produced it. Thus, the very negative character of Dadabhai's economic approach--which made him a prophet of doom in comparison with Ranade's optimistic outlook that had compelled the latter to drop the drain theory after initially sponsoring it in 1872 also enabled him to give as early as 19o4 a call that must have appeared at the time to be utterly negative and 'hopeless'- the call for self-government.

The revolutionary political implications of the drain theory were visible not only to its proponents but even more so to its critics. As early as 1886, Randolph Churchill, the Secretary of State for India, had pointed out that

The impatience of the new taxation, which would have to be borne wholly as a consequence of foreign rule imposed on the country, and virtually to meet additions to charges arising outside the country, would constitute a political danger, the real magnitude of which it is to be feared is not at all appreciated by persons who have no knowledge or concern in the Government of India, but which those responsible for that Government have long regarded as of the most serious order.235

Similarly, J. D. Rees in his book, The Real India, took Dadabhai to task for leaving out of account ‘the fact that without the home charges there could be no British Government in India'.236 Several Indians also opposed a vigorous propagation of the drain theory for the reason that it would sooner or later lead to a premature demand for political independence.237 This was perhaps the main reason why Ranade opposed the tendency to emphasise the drain too much.

To sum up: the drain theory was revolutionary in its political implications; it brought the question of political power to the centre of the stage; its acceptance not only brought the inherent political conflict between Britain and India to the surface but made the political domination of the former over the latter impermissible. This also explains why while nationalist criticism of British economic policy in several other respects and the consequent remedies suggested by the national leaders could be accepted by even the most ardent of British imperialists, the existence of the drain would not be accepted by even the most radical of them.

The drain theory also helped politicalise the public life of India by raising economic issues to a political level; for while other economic issues could possibly be resolved by economic remedies and could, therefore, under certain circumstances, be kept within the realm of economic discussion, the drain could find only a political solution. The drain theory, thus, also tended to replace political passivity by political action.

The drain theory possessed the great political merit of easily becoming a current coin among the common masses; it was so simple and easy a concept that even a peasant could readily grasp it. A nationalist orator discoursing on the drain could get a quick response of understanding from the most illiterate of audiences. Money being transferred from one country to another was the most easily understood of the theories of economic exploitation since it was so akin to their daily experience; a peasant listening to an attack upon the drain could as good as feel money slipping out of his own pocket. No other idea could arouse people more than the thought that they were being taxed so that others in far off lands might live in comfort. The drain theory consequently had the quality of becoming the rallying cry of a nation of peasants. 'No drain' was the type of slogan that all successful revolutions need and possess-—it didn't have to be proved by sophisticated and complex arguments but had, instead, a sort of immanent quality about it; it was practically self-evident. And so the belief that India was a sone ki chidya (the land of gold) that had slowly been drained of all its wealth till it was reduced to the present condition of poverty and destitution became the most powerful and popular of nationalist grievances during the years when Indian nationalism was at its zenith. It was therefore not fortuitous that the drain theory was attacked by the officials, as well as by non-official British writers, with a vigour and consistency which was not the fate of any other nationalist theory or demand and that its chief theoretician and propagator, Dadabhai Naoroji, whose professions of loyalty to British rule were loudest of all and whose faith in British goodness was deepest of all, was condemned as a visionary, an extremist,238 and perhaps even a hypocrite who was hiddenly

238. See, for example, Naoroji, Speeches, Appendix pp. 77-8.

disloyal.239 But, on the other hand, at a .time when nearly all the great moderate leaders of the 19th century, like Ranade, Pherozeshah Mehta and Gokhale, came to be temporarily looked down upon and even ridiculed as petition wallahs by the nationalistically aroused youngmen of the Gandhian era, Dadabhai was still remembered and respected by them as the father of Indian nationalism.

# CHAPTER XIV

# Indian Political Economy

Theory is only enlarged Practice, Practice is Theory 'studied in its relation to proximate causes.

— JUSTICE RANADE

The given conditions of society and the prevailing conceptions regarding its future and contemporary interest, determined the economic ideas that received the approbation of thinkers and the acceptance of statesmen.

— G. SUBRAMANIYA IYER

During the period under study, some of the Indian national leaders evolved the concept of Indian Political Economy' which was fully worked out in a scientific form by Justice Ranade in his classic address on 'Indian Political Economy', delivered at the Deccan College, Poona, in 1892.1 His basic formulations are too well known in Indian economic literature to need an elaborate statement here.2 What is perhaps not so well known is the wide scale on which they were subscribed to in the ranks of the Indian national leadership. Ranade's views on the subject may be summarised as follows:

The claim that the postulates of Classical political economy were universally and absolutely and demonstrably true for all times and places and for all stages of economic development was far from true.3 Classical economics was based on assumptions which were not universally applicable; they had been born out of specific conditions of England; and, in fact, they were true of no existing society.4 In particular, they had hardly any validity in a backward, stagnant agricultural country like India where not competition but status and custom and tradition ruled.5 Secondly, the past practice of British statesmen and the contemporary practice of other nations did not at all conform to the precepts or assumptions of Classical economics.6 As a matter of fact, even in India the British did not strictly adhere to these precepts in giving guarantees to railways and help to plantation industries or in claiming state ownership over land or in trying to preserve through legislation petit culture by tenants.7 Thirdly, the scientific claims of Classical economics had been seriously questioned by European and American economists8 and were under attack even by several English economists;9 moreover, some of the Classical economists themselves had been at pains to avoid making claims of absoluteness and universality for their science.1o Fourthly, Classical economics might at the most explain static economic conditions but could hardly cope with the processes of dynamic economic development.11 Keeping all these factors in view, the conclusion was inescapable that the principles or laws of economics were not abstract and universal like those of physics and mathematics but were relative and historically conditioned and derived; they differed in time and space.1² Hence, the specific stage of economic development of a people and not abstract economic theories should be the decisive factor in determining economic policies for a country.

It may be noted that Ranade did not deny the value of economic theory or the validity of economics as a science.13 On the contrary, he specifically repudiated those who would reduce it to an Art, to 'the position of a rule of thumb'.14 He only wanted to make it more ‘subtle and scientific' by basing it on practice.15 He said that economics being a social science its principles were to be derived historically through the study of specific economic activities and not deductively so that they were closely linked with historical experience, practical observation, and social reality. Or, as D. G. Karve has put it, ‘a comprehensive, sociological and constructive view of economics was alone acceptable to Ranade'.i6 In 1881, Ranade had criticised English economists for looking at the question of Free Trade 'solely from the economical point of view' and ignoring 'the political and social elements .which enter into the question', and averred that the economic aspect ought to be subordinated to the higher interests and aspirations of a people ‘if political economy is to be anything more than schoolmen's metaphysics'. In fact, he pointed out, that is why one talked of ‘applied, i.e. political economy' and not of 'pure economics'.17 In this respect Ranade remained for once true to the practice of Classical economists and more often than not referred to economics as political economy.

In course of time Ranade's critique of Classical economics led to the foundation of the 'national' or 'Indian' school of political economy. This development was, we believe, in the main the product of two factors: (a) Ranade's conclusion derived from a belief in the relativity of economic doctrines, in their close dependence on social reality, and in their quality of reflecting economic practice.-that since India's economic interests and conditions differed from England's the principles of economics applicable to it must also be different;18 and (b) the acceptance of Ranade's premises and conclusions by a large number of other nationalist contemporaries.

Thus, as early as 1877, K. T. Telang quoted the English economist T. E. Cliffe-Leslie to show that the doctrines of political economy were not true of all countries alike.18a He also said that in deciding practical questions principles of economics should form only one of the considerations, other considerations of social and political welfare being equally important.19 In his paper on ‘The Poverty of India', Dadabhai argued that political economy might be ‘all right in a Nativegoverned country', but was not strictly applicable to a dependent country which had to make 'home remittances and charges'.2o Prithwis Chandra Ray too condemned any attempt 'to take the abstract principles of political economy and apply them cut and dried in a country, without considering its sociological conditions', and cited the experience of America, Australia, and the Continent of Europe as a proof of the error of the 'complacent dogmatism'of English economists.2i G. V. Joshi did not deal directly with economic theory, but his treatment of the questions of the role of the state, Free Trade, etc. clearly brings out his close adherence to the fundamental theoretical framework of Ranade.2z R.C. Dutt did not comment on economic theory; he did, however, notice that Ricardo, Cobden, Bright, and Robert Peel had ignored the heavy duties on Indian goods entering England at the very time when they were agitating for Free Trade in England. Nor did he fail to praise European economists for taking ‘a more unbiased view of the situation'and for unerringly pointing to the double standard practised by English statesmen at home and in India.23

G. Subramaniya Iyer dealt at length with problems of political economy in his work, Some Economic Aspects of British Rule in India, to which he attached a long Appendix on 'English Economists and India'. Even though he was obviously following in the footsteps of Ranade, his treatment of the question is historically significant. Dealing with the question of Free Trade, he pointed to the protectionist policies of Germany and the U.S.A. to show that economic policies of nations were determined by national needs and not by economic theories, and remarked that the theory of Free Trade suited England because of its peculiar conditions and that, on the other hand, Indian conditions resembled those of Russia and the U.S.A. in this respect.24 Coming to problems of theory, he said that Classical economics was 'one-sided and imperfect' and not applicable to other countries.25 Moreover, he remarked echoing Ranade —'the truths of economic science are not universally applicable, irrespective of particular stages of national growth and of peculiar environments and conditions.'z6 He also pointed out that European political economy had never stood still; it had undergone 'steady changes in its conceptions, theories, and practical application, since the dissolution of medieval times'.27 Adam Smith himself belonged to a particular point of time.28 Stressing the social origins of economic thought, G. S. Iyer wrote: 'The given conditions of society and the prevailing conceptions regarding its future and contemporary interest, determined the economic ideas that received the approbation of thinkers and the acceptance of statesmen.'It was therefore- clear to him that the principles of orthodox economics had to be modified when applied to India since 'the economic interests of India and the conditions under which they have to be safeguarded' were different from those of Europe and America.29 He also noted that the practice of the English in India provided many exceptions to their adherence to orthodox economics; unfortunately, however, he pointed out, such departures from dogma were made only 'to uphold and protect the interests of the ascendant class'.3o

The relativity of economic doctrines and of their application to the peculiar conditions of India was also sometimes asserted at the popular level of the press. Thus, for example, the Amrita Bazar Patrika had written, as early as 1 December 187o, that 'the condition of India is in many respects peculiar and the same law which holds good in other countries may not hold good here'. The Hindu of 7 December 1883 observed : 'In the application of the European economic laws to Eastern countries, the special conditions must be taken into consideration....Your political economy is not after all an end in itself.'The Mahratta of 2 April 1899 criticised Adam Smith and Cobden for trying to make of political economy a science like chemistry. They started out rightly, it said, with the practical question how to increase the wealth of England. Unfortunately, in their hands the question 'gradually lost its specific nature and the change from particular to the general was easy and swift'. In any case, it asserted, the economic condition of India, being different from that of England, 'calls out for the application of a different set of principles'. It further declared : 'India is not to be sold for the fads of economic doctrinaires'; and asked the Government of India to 'proclaim once for all that it is not going to borrow its economics from England'. In conclusion it pleaded for 'the formation of national economics'.31 Bipin Chandra Pal's New India also dilated, in its issue dated 21 April 19o2, on the relativity of economic doctrines on account of 'the wide divergence that exists between one people and another both in their social structures, and in their climatic and historic environments', and, pointing to the social roots of economic theory, observed that economic ideas were 'the result of particular industrial conditions, existing in any country or at any time, and must, therefore, vary from age to age, and between one country and another'.32 Many other nationalist leaders referred to the relativity of economic principles in the course of the agitation against the application of Free Trade principles to India.33

Most of the Indian leaders cited above made a distinct advance over Ranade's position in one respect. They were able to discern that it was in its political condition that India differed most not only from England but also from the other nations of Europe and America, for India's economy and finances were controlled by an alien power.34

## I. ROLE OF THE STATE

The laissez-faire theory of the functions of the state was one of the tenets of Classical economics that was challenged vigorously and consistently by the leading nationalist economists. They rejected the idea that the state should confine its activities to the maintenance of law and order35 and pleaded for the widening of the horizons of state activity so as to encompass all matters in which individual effort was likely to be less effective than national effort.36 The state should in this respect act as the collective organ of the national will for national purposes.37 They further maintained that in an economically backward country like India the obligation on the state to act as the guardian of national economic and industrial interests was even greater than in the advanced countries of the West, since it had to help people overcome inherited weaknesses, their own inertia and inferior competitive position vis-a-vis the powerful foreigner.38 G. V. Joshi, in particular, felt that it was the duty of the state in India to so regulate the inexorable working of economic laws of competition and control and guide the inevitable transition from handicrafts to modern industries as to minimise the resultant economic dislocation and misery.39 There was, however, some difference of opinion between Justice Ranade and G. V. Joshi regarding the limits to which state' intervention in economic affairs could go. Ranade opposed the setting of any theoretical limits and said that the national needs should be the only criterion. 'The question is one of time, fitness, and expediency, not one of liberty and rights', he said.4o G. V. Joshi, on the other hand, believed that the state action was an extraordinary measure and should, therefore, be hedged in with restrictions lest instead of aiding private effort it suppressed and even replaced it.41

In order to justify the right of the state to interfere in economic affairs, Ranade and G. Subramaniya Iyer also attacked the doctrine of the social supremacy of the ‘individual and his self-interest that lay at the heart of the concepts like the ‘economic man' and self-regulating economy, and propounded in its place the doctrine of the primacy of collective welfare over individual interests. Ranade put this view as follows:

Modern Thought is nearing to the conclusion that the centre round which the Theory should revolve, that the true centre is the Body Politic of which that Individual is a Member, and that Collective Defence and Well-being, Social Education and Discipline, and the Duties, and not merely the Interests, of men, must be taken into account, if the Theory is not to be merely Utopian.42

Similarly, G. S. Iyer criticised Adam Smith for treating the individual ‘as a purely egoistical force, working uniformly in the direction of private gain' and not paying due regard to the fact that man should 'cultivate sympathy with his neighbours, with his nation, and with humanity at large'. Echoing the German historical school, he said :

...the immediate private interest of the separate members of the community will not lead to the highest good of the whole, and is frequently opposed to it....On the maintenance of the unity and progress of the nation depend the security, well-being and civilization of the individual, and private economic interests, like all others, must be subordinated to the completion and strengthening of the nationality.43

G. V. Joshi made the interesting point in this context that the philosophy of laissez faire had arisen in England as a reaction to the state helping one section of the community at the cost of another. He would concede that objection might be rightly taken where the state was found 'favouring a class and promoting only sectional, instead of national, interests'. But the case was quite different in India, where, he said, 'there is no such conflict of class-interests; the classes are not divided, as elsewhere, by hard lines'. Here the state was not being asked to help onę class against another nor to favour the capitalist class as such; the state was being asked ‘to help the entire community in its present helplessness'. All the classes of Indian society were suffering at the hands of competition by powerful foreigners; all of them were equally interested in the rapid economic development of the country.44

Basing themselves on the twin doctrines of the state's right to interfere in economic matters and the predominance of communal welfare over individual interest, the Indian leaders demanded that the Government of India should follow a policy of direct, deliberate, and systematic promotion of industrial development which was in their eyes the sine qua non of nation's economic welfare.45 Many of them also asked for various forms of state assistance to agriculture.46 Ranade applied this doctrine of the state being the guardian of the entire community to fields where there was need for ‘protection of the weak against the strong'.47 He supported legislation to protect tenants and to regulate money-lending.48 He also advocated a more equitable distribution of national wealth.49 Similarly, G. S. Iyer advocated state intervention in favour of the workers on the ground that the state had the responsibility of protecting the weaker side in the unequal struggle between labour and capital.5o It may, however, be repeated here that quite a few other Indians took strong objection to any state interference with 'the quiet working of private industrial enterprise'.51

A note of warning may be sounded at this stage. Care should be taken not to confuse the nationalist economists' theoretical defence of state interference in economic affairs and their demand for state aid to industry — which might in some cases even take the form of direct state enterprise —with a desire for socialism or even for a welfare state.52 Far from advocating socialism or even state capitalism, these economists merely wanted the state to provide an impetus to and give a helping hand in the transition of the agrarian economy towards industrial capitalism — all they desired was to induce the state to make up the economic deficiencies of private enterprise in a backward country. That this was the role assigned to state aid by Ranade is clearly brought out by a perusal of his essays on Netherlands India and the Culture System', 'Iron Industry — Pioneer Attempts', and 'Industrial Conference'.53 Light is also shed on his attitude by a little-known act of his in 1886. While he talked in general of producing ‘in State Factories all products of skill which the" State Departments require in the way of Stores',s4 in the course of his minute of dissent to the Report of the Finance Committee of 1886 he strongly objected to the Committee's proposal for removal of restrictions on jail manufactures as it 'encourages the state officials with the help of public funds to bear down all private competition'. In particular, he criticised the utilisation of jails 'to drive steam machinery and work presses, and tents, and paper, and carpets with the help of State funds'. In other words, he objected to jails being used to manufacture goods which the Indian private enterprise also produced.ss

G. V. Joshi repudiated any suggestion of state socialism or state capitalism even more strongly and explicitly. Thus he wrote in 189o: 'Our scheme emphatically is not a scheme of State-socialism, such, for instance, as was attempted with fatal ill-success by the Provisional Government in 1848.'s6 Firstly, he repeatedly remarked that the only objective of state action was to redress the balance in the unequal struggle of the resourceless Indian entrepreneur with the powerful and go-ahead foreigner' so that the former was not nipped in the bud'.57 Secondly, he stressed that industrial .development was basically the function and prerogative of private effort, the state's role being temporary and limited to that of preparing the private entrepreneur to assume independent responsibility. As soon as the latter eventuality was realised the policy of state aid would lose its raison d'etre and would have to be given up. It would in fact be wrong at that stage for the state to try to monopolise the industrial field.58 Thirdly, apprehending that state aid might 'degenerate into aggressive State action, supplanting private effort', he proposed surrounding it with strict 'limitations of scope, direction, and time'.59 Lastly, he criticised the government for attempting in respect of irrigation, forest conservancy, and taqavi allowances 'to accomplish by its own agency the work which private native enterprise should be trained and encouraged and even subsidised if necessary to carry out'.6o

## II. LAND REVENUE: A TAX OR RENT?

Another field in which the Indian leaders questioned the official theoretical framework was that of land revenue administration. The main points of difference related to the nature of land revenue and the ultimate ownership of agricultural land. The dominant view, among the Indian administrators was that in India the state was, since times immemorial, the ultimate owner of the soil and the cultivators or other landholders were in essence its tenants; in consequence the land revenue paid by the landholders to the government was in the nature of rent and not a tax.61 The nationalist economists and other leaders repudiated this contention in toto. They said that the Indian landholder was as much a private owner of his land as a landholder in any other part of the world, and that to assert the contrary was to misinterpret the past and ignore even the principles underlying the existing land tenures. They therefore maintained that what the government charged as land revenue was a tax that it collected for its needs in the manner of governments in other countries.62 Interestingly enough, while the official spokesmen often stressed that in claiming to be the universal lords of the soil the British rulers were merely following the practice of the previous rulers of the land, G. V. Joshi insisted that the claim was put up because 'Western Theories are recklessly applied to the facts of Indian life in all their logical rigours'.63

Ranade and Joshi also attacked the Ricardian basis of the Indian systei of land revenue. Underlying the official ideas. and practice was the assumption that the tenant of the state or the zamindar in India was a capitalist farmer who paid economic rent,64 that is the price differential between the produce of superior land and the produce of the poorest land at the margin of cultivation on which the cost of production was just covered by its produce. The cost of cultivation under this definition covered the cultivator's labour costs as well as his capital costs at the prevailing rate of profit.65 It was clear that according to this theory of rent the state as the landlord in India could absorb the whole of this economic rent as land revenue without raising the cost of production and without affecting prices or wages which were determined by the cost of production on the marginal land.66 Ranade and Joshi did not criticise the Ricardian theory of rent as such, but they denied that it was valid in India. 'In classical political economy the rent of a capitalist farmer was determined by the competition of capital, the size of the population, and the varying fertility of the soil.'67 Ranade's objection was that, since the state had become the sole landlord in the country, the cultivator paid not a competitive but a monopoly rent which the government was in a position to pitch as high as it liked and which frequently encroached upon the profits and wages of the cultivator.68 Joshi's objection was even more fundamental. He pointed out that Ricardo's theory assumed the existence of capitalist farmers who just did not exist in India. The Indian cultivator, both in the zamindari and the ryotwari areas, was more in the nature of a cottier tenant than a capitalist farmer moved by considerations of economic profit. According to him, there were two basic factors in the situation : (a) the immediate object of the cultivator was sheer subsistence and not profit on capital invested; and (b) there was intense competition for land among the cultivators because of the grø ing population, gradual disappearance of cultivable waste, a 'failure of non-agricultural industries'. In consequence the ryot, driven by 'cruel economic necessity' and sooner than part with his only source of living—his patch of landand become a landless labourer, agreed to almost any terms imposed by :. state or the private landlord even if it meant living belr subsistence level.69 I9T

Ranade and Joshi also criticised the theory of the unearntT increment that was a natural corollary of Ricardo's theory :c rent. According to this theory if the rent of a piece of la increased by more than the prevailing rate of profit on capil that might have in the meantime been invested in it then th difference between the two formed an unearned increment to rent, which would be the product of the genęral fall in the rat of profit and the general progress of the community and whic would, therefore, legitimately belong to society rather than the owner of land who would tend to appropriate it para: cally.7o Ranade and Joshi argued that the application of tli. theory in the Indian system of land revenue assumed a dynami upward movement of society and economy and rent but a stat situation in landownership. But there was no warrant for thi; assumption in actuality. Unlike in England, where land cont nued in the possession of the same family for generations, i India land was constantly changing hands with the result tha each incoming owner paid for it at its full current value—bot earned and unearnedand the unearned income of the p? totally disappeared in the process.71 Moreover, remarked Jos' in their search for the unearned income the Indian reve.

"thorities often took recourse to ‘forced increment' and in the ocess encroached on the very livelihood of the cultivator.72

## II. FREE TRADE VS PROTECTION

m the beginning the nationalists were deeply interested in question of Free Trade vs Protection. As early as 1877, K. T. lang had remarked in the course of a long paper on 'Free 0 ade and Protection—from an Indian Point of View': 'It is subject so intimately connected with the whole future of our intry—no less in its social and political than in its industrial

pects—that it is impossible to exaggerate the importance of aving correct views upon it.'73

The nationalist leaders with near unanimity demanded that n the interest of healthy economic development the Governent of India should help the nascent industries of the country granting them protection.74 Thus, for example, G. Subrama-¿ Iyer wrote in 1903:

1 To bring into existence a variety of non-agricultural occupations is the most paramount want of the country and this can be done... by the adoption of a policy of Protection....

so long as the present so-called Free Trade and unequal competition continue, India has no hope of a future which will be distinguished by material prosperity.... To preserve th foodgrains and raw materials that she produces for consumption in her own domestic markets, by taxing their exports, and to prevent the import of competitive manufactures ov (RN protective duties in view to the growth of indigenous indes tries—this is the policy that alone will save India.75

Strangely enough, however, the Indian National Congress did not demand protection even once during the first 2o years of its existence. This was perhaps partly in deference to its Radical allies in England and partly due to its reluctance to put forward a demand that was, in view of Britain's stern adherence to Free Trade, obviously 'unrealisable' politically.76

Seeing that the doctrine of Free Trade was a major hindrance in the path of industrial progress through protection, the nationalist leaders set out to disprove its validity in Indian conditions and to show that, on the other hand, protection would be useful and not harmful to the long-range interests of India. The earliest and the most scientific effort in the direction was made by K.T. Telang in 1877 in the paper referred to earlier. Many later writers seem to have relied on this work.

The main nationalist argument against Free Trade was its unsuitability to India.77 The Indian leaders said that it might have benefited England, but in India it had only caused, and was causing, industrial havoc.78 They further maintained that Free Trade could exist only among equals; between India and England it was something like ‘a race between a starving, exhausting (sic!) invalid, and a strong man with a horse to ride on'.79

Some of the Indian leaders also refuted the assertion that protection restricted the freedom of the consumer and the liberty of the entrepreneur. They said that liberty and freedom could not possibly mean freedom to foreigners to ruin India's nascent enterprises through unlimited competition and that, in fact, in Indian conditions real liberty would come only through protection and 'artificial nourishment', while Free Trade meant giving protection to England, the stronger party.8o As far as the cost of protection to the consumer was concerned, they were convinced that the nation would be quite willing to bear it especially since the long-term benefits of fall in prices, increase in wages and employment, and general growth of national income would far outweigh the cost.81 Telang also had little difficulty in showing that in India protection would not lead to the diversion of capital from its natural channels but would instead help mobilise the country's idle hoards.8z As far as labour was concerned, he continued, the consequences of protection and the resultant growth of industry would be entirely beneficial; it would lead to an increase in the price of labour because of the increased demand for it. This led him to say that even if protection did diminish the aggregate production of a country it would still be desirable in a country like India in' order to secure a more equitable distribution of national income. ‘After all,'he wrote, ‘it does not do much good to a country to have a few bloated fortunes in the midst of a dead level of indigence here and there chequered with squalid misery. It is better, I humbly conceive, to have fewer large fortunes if the population at large be at the same time removed above the needs of starvation and misery.'83

Several Indian economists questioned the chief premise of the Classical economists that Free Trade led, through the working of comparative costs, to the most efficient geographical division of labour. They refused to accept as ‘almost a Providential dispensation' any division of labour that would consign India for ever to be a producer of raw materials, and rejected the assumption that 'India should continue to export the raw material to England and import the manufactured goods from England'.84 They asserted that India was by its history, geography, and resources eminently fitted to be a great manufacturing country.85 In any case, temporary protection was needed to test whether or not certain industries were better suited to new countries than to their old habitats.86 Moreover, there could be no fair and mutually beneficial division of labour till the full capacities of every nation were known and developed. To have universal Free Trade just at the moment would stereotype the existing pattern of international division of labour and mean that those countries which had developed earlier would for ever remain industrially advanced while others like India would be further reduced to an agrarian status pure and simple.87 In this context, K. T. Telang warned against a country being reduced to a single-industry country since any change in fashions, tastes, and needs affecting its products would spell disaster for it.88

Several of the Indian leaders also argued that protection to infant industries for a limited period of time was not opposed. even to the tenets of laissez faire, and pointed to the writings. of John Stuart Mill as having approved of protection in the type of situation prevailing in India.89 In fact, many of them claimed to be free traders in principle who were favouring protection for India as a special case.9o

Some of them brought forward a very ingenious argument to prove the correctness and justice of protection in India's case. Referring to the heavy duties imposed on the import of Indian manufactures in England up to the middle of the 19th century K. T. Telang, for example, asserted that 'the agency employed to destroy our indigenous manufactures was this very Protection' and claimed that all the Indians asked for was that 'the same instrument which was used as a sword against us should now be used as a shield for us'.91 Similarly, R. C. Dutt remarked caustically: 'But in India the Manufacturing Power of the people was stamped out by protection against her industries, and then free trade was forced on her so as to prevent a. revival.'92

To strengthen their case for protection, the Indian leaders appealed to the practice of nations as against the theories of political economy and pointed out that nearly all the free nations of Europe and America and Britain's own colonies like Canada and Australia had protected their newly started industries whenever these were threatened by foreign competitors.93 They also very acutely noted that England itself had reared its modern industries under the fostering care of protection during the last decades of the i8th century and the opening decades of the 19th century, and that it was only after its industrial machine had been properly geared up for successful competition abroad that it had proclaimed to the world the doctrine of Free Trade.94 Also, R. C. Dutt and G. Subramaniya Iyer did not fail to notice that while the British economists like Adam Smith had been loudly preaching the doctrine of Free Trade from the end of the i8th century onwards, the British statesmen turned free traders much later and only when it suited Britain.95 Many of the nationalist leaders went to the extent of saying that the British did not sincerely believe in the principles of Free Trade and were using them as a cover-or, as G. V. Joshi put it, a 'metaphysical framework'—to push their products in India and to prevent the growth of an industrial rival.96

In spite of their criticism of Free Trade and advocacy of a protective umbrella for industries, none of the prominent nationalist leaders adopted a doctrinaire position in favour of protection. Their attitude in the matter was highly eclectic. This was but natural for their rejection of Free Trade was also not doctrinaire. As in case of their other economic attitudes, their tariff policy was not the product of general principles of economics but sprang from, and was closely related to, concrete economic issues. This eclecticism, inherent in their very defence of protection, was sometimes clearly articulated. For instance, R. C. Dutt wrote in 19o1 : 'I do not pin my faith to Free Trade, and I do not pin my faith to Protection. I hold that the policy most conducive to the prosperity and happiness of the people of India is the policy which should be adopted for India.'97 In fact, as some of the nationalist leaders openly stated during the first years of the 2oth century, what they desired was not so much protection as the right to frame an independent tariff policy.98 At this time a few Indians also began to discriminate between different types of protection and see clearly that the wrong-type of protection could result in a large scale influx of foreign capital which would be able to use protection to monopolise the industrial field. Thus the editor of the Hindustan Review and Kayastha Samachar wrote in November 1903:

... with the present political and economic subjection of India to England, the adoption of a policy of Protection is most likely to be abused in favour of the Anglo-Indian producers and alien exploiters generally and... the. struggle will be between the British capitalist and the foreign capitalist and not between the Indians on the one hand and all aliens on the other.99

## Similarly, the Hindu of 13 October 1903 observed :

. . . there is no less grave danger of the interests of the Indian people being dominated by those of Europeans who have invested their capital in this country. A boon which is intended for India may fall really to the lot of the European planters and merchants.... there is an increasing tendency among certain class of people to speak of the interests of India as though they are the interests of the small number of Europeans who are engaged in exploiting the country.1oo

## IV. SOME OTHER OBSERVATIONS

In conclusion attention may be drawn to a few other important aspects of the doctrinal outlook of the nationalist economists.

An important aspect of the economic thinking of the nationalists was its social orientation. As has been shown above, the nationalist economists realised that economic ideas. and policies had to be subordinated to socially derived goals and perspectives. In fact the entire economic thought of the nationalists was pervaded by this understanding; and this notion of subjecting economic thinking and policy to socially integrated goals perhaps forms the specific" core of what is. known as Indian economics. This view of economic ideas and policy also established from the very beginning close interrelation between Indian economics and politics. It enabled the nationalist leaders to link nearly every important economic question with the politically dependent status of the country and to take note of the most imporant political and economic aspect of the Indian reality—that India was being ruled by a foreign power for the purposes of economic exploitation.

Apart from this, while their economic thinking was ahead of contemporary English thinking and in tune with thinking in Europe and America—in stressing that the same propositions of economics could not be applied to countries at different stages of economic development and that for each country these propositions should be formulated in the context of the general economic needs of the country concerned —it did not yet result in the emergence of any new 'Indian system of economic ideas or theories.1oi In spite of the use of adjectives like 'Indian' and 'national' before their economic thought, the Indian nationalist economists did not discover any new economic laws which would arise out of, and be applicable only to, the specific and peculiar conditions of India. What we get from them is an amalgam of the ideas of Classical economists and their later-day critics. It might almost be said that Indian Political Economy was more an attitude of mind, an approach towards Indian economic problems, and a method of economic reasoning than a system of economic thought.

This was largely due to the fact that the nationalist economists' interest in economic theory was secondary or derivative. They were not professional economists interested in developing economic theory or in evolving an Indian version of it. They, therefore, made no attempt to grapple with theory per se. Their comments on economic theory have, in fact, to be gleaned from a vast mass of writing on contemporary economic problems. Their political economy arose out of a very practical consideration: because the rulers of India used the authority of Classical economics to oppose nationalist demands, the nationalist leaders in turn felt it necessary to challenge the universality of Classical economics and its applicability to Indian conditions. This rather mundane origin of their economic thought was sometimes consciously expressed by them. Thus they said that they had to demolish the doctrine of laissez faire functions of the state as it came in the way of state assistance to industries,1o2 the Ricardian theory of rent because it was used to justify the high pitch of land revenue, to reject their demand for the permanent settlement of land revenue, and to deny their allegation that the level of taxation in India was very high,1o3 the doctrine of Free Trade because it was in part responsible for the repeal of cotton import duties, imposition of cottón excise duties, and the refusal to grant protection to nascent industries.104

But even though there was nothing new in the economic thought of the nationalists, mere statement of the Indian point of view and adaptation of European thought to Indian questions. had an importance of its own. After all the very act of choosing between different theoretical positions constitutes an act of theorising and is even theoretically significant. The Indian leaders revealed that, though the products of English education, they were at no stage overwhelmed by it and were not afraid of attacking the shibboleths governing the English mind. Moreover, they also deserve credit for taking a comprehensive and integrated view of economic science. Though they did not develop a consistent economic theory or political economy of growth, they did do the next best thing—tliey gave a consistent, integrated, interrelated picture of Indian economy, its diseases, their relation to the alien rule, and the renedies to be applied. We may go so far as to say that they tried to evolve some sort of political economy of growth in which developments in industry, foreign trade, fiscal policy, and agriculture were closely intertwined and which was geared first of all to rapid industrialisation of the country. In this connection, it may also be noted that the relativity of economic principles and the notion of Indian 'exceptionalism' were utilised by the early nationalist leaders not to justify or conserve the purely indigenous or pre-modern pattern of Indian economy but to accelerate the speed of its modernisation. Classical economics was rejected not because it was disrupting the age-old pattern of Indian social life but because its rigid and indiscriminate application tended to perpetuate the 'old legacies and inherited weaknesses',105 and 'the ancient bondage of Feudalism and status’;1o6 and an 'Indian Political Economy' was brought forth to promote the rapid transformation and modernisation of the national economic life.

# CHAPTER XV

# Economic Nationalism

The fact is, you cannot both be a conquering nation and a benevolent nation at the same time. D-l Fehruarv 1802 — Amrita Bazar Patrika, 12 February 1892

You announce yourself as a sincere supporter of British rule; you vehemently denounce the conditions and consequences which are inseparable from the maintenance of that rule. GEORGE HAMILTON CEORGE HAMILTON

## I. ANALYSIS OF THE NATURE AND PURPOSE OF BRITISH RULE IN INDIA

The attitude that the Indian national leadership of the period under study adopted towards British rule was ultimately determined by their understanding of its nature and purpose. In the case of most of them this understanding was not derived from theoretical reasoning or a priori assumptions: practice was the hard school in which they were brought up. One such field of their education was that of economic policies. Debate and discussion between Indians and Indians, on the one hand, and between Indians and their rulers, on the other, over nearly every economic issue that arose in contemporary administration and politics had some bearing on this basic political understanding. In the end, the multifarious controversies over economic policies, and, in particular, over the causes of India's poverty and the consequent remedies, led large sections of the nationalist leadership to believe—sometimes hesitantly and even confusedly, as in the case of G. K. Gokhale, G. V. Joshi,

Surendranath Banerjea, D. E. Wacha, and R. C. Dutt, but often in a clear-cut manner, as in the case of Dadabhai Naoroji, B.G. Tilak, G. Subramaniya Iyer, the Amrita Bazar Patrika, the Mahratta, and numerous other nationalist papers—that, on the whole, British rule was economically injurious to India and that perhaps it was designedly so.

To many of the Indian leaders, particularly to those who later came to be known as Moderates, British rule held for long a great promise. They were dazzled by the initial impact on India of Britain, the most advanced nation of the contemporary world. To them, law and order and a modern centralised administration, coming as they did after the nearpolitical anarchy of the 18th and early 19th centuries, the spread of modern education and through its medium of Western democratic thought and enlightenment, the introduction of the freedom of speech and the Press and of social liberty, but perhaps most of all the process of the welding of the people of India into one common nationality, the consequent growth all over the country of the feeling of belonging to one common entity, and the birth of a new political life—all seemed to follow in the wake of, and were therefore the accomplishments of, British Raj and heralded the coming dawn. In the realm of economics, it was the prospect of rapid industrial development that attracted them. Western science and technique and economic organisation and the example of vigorous European enterprise, they hoped, would reclaim the country from the slough of economic backwardness and stagnation. The railways, roads, and canals, the link with the flourishing markets of the world, the early textile industry, and the foreign commercial, industrial, and plantation enterprises appeared to be a preparation for, and a prelude to, the coming industrial revolution whose first signs were already visible.i

It was not as if the early nationalists were not aware of the prevalence of poverty and other economic disabilities.
